COMMENT: A massive demonstration of support for President Houphouet-Boigny was organized by the P.D.C.I. in Abidjan on Sept. 28
Student demonstration at the University of Abidjan, Jan 22 1967, several hundred students arrested
Student demonstration
Arrest of some 100 students who had protested on Feb. 9, 1982

The country's longest strike, involving teachers in secondary and higher education, doctors, pharmacists, dentists and veterinary surgeons, began on April 18, 1983, and was ended on May 3 by a presidential decree
Attacks on Burkinabe citizens in the Ivory Coast
Occupation of Abidjan cathedral on Feb. 23, with students demonstrators calling on Houphouët-Boigny to resign
Street protests in February and March against new economic plans
Demonstrations in the capital calling for the President's resignation
Protesters on April 5 disrupted a march organized to show support for the President

On May 16 some 900 army conscripts took control of the international airport at Abidjan, the capital, for 12 hours in protest at low levels of pay and poor employment prospects. Another group of conscripts had on May 14 occupied the national radio station
A police officers' strike on May 21 was followed by similar action by firemen and customs officers
Voting took place in an atmosphere of tension, in Abidjan, angry FPI supporters attacked polling booths and clashed with police, alleging blatant ballot-rigging
Student and opposition protest recurred during May and June. The authorities denied newspaper reports that four had been killed
PDCI rally
PDCI rally
Disaffected junior soldiers had attempted a coup d'état
Three days of student-initiated demonstrations on Feb. 4-6 led to confrontations between the military and students
More than 100 people were arrested in Abidjan, on Feb. 18 as a 20,000-strong demonstration degenerated into a riot of unprecedented violence
On March 29, 45 members of the elite corps took a commander and two non-commissioned officers hostage at the Presidential Palace in Abidjan
Relations with Ghana deteriorated after 35 people, mostly Ghanaians, died in riots which erupted in Abidjan in early November

Around 10,000 demonstrators took to the streets of Abidjan on May 27 to demand the withdrawal of the controversial electoral code which imposed strict nationality and residential requirements on presidential candidates
FPI rally
Police and protesters clashed frequently: on Sept. 29, protesters who had erected barricades and road blocks in the Yopougon district of Abidjan were dispersed by police using tear gas and batons
Wave of violence and looting in Abidjan

A provincial court decision to revoke the Ivorian nationality of the main opposition contender in the October 2000 presidential election, Alassane Ouattara, led to violent clashes on Oct. 27 between the police and opposition supporters, in Abidjan
President Henri Konan Bédié was overthrown by a former chief of general staff of the armed forces, Gen. Robert Guëi, in a virtually bloodless military coup carried out over Dec. 24-25

A mutiny on July 4-5 by troops demanding bonuses for the part they played in the December 1999 military coup. ...three soldiers were killed in a clash between the mutineers and forces loyal to the government
It was reported on Sep. 19 that armed assailants had stormed the official residence of President Robert Guëi in Abidjan. Heavy fighting ensued, resulting in at least 10 deaths
A bomb explosion in the capital, Abidjan

Protest against President Guëi. On Oct. 25 an increasing number of soldiers and security personnel joined the demonstrators and they succeeded in occupying the state-run television station.  The situation deteriorated rapidly, with fighting breaking out b

A mass grave was uncovered in Yopougon [Abidjan suburb], which allegedly contained bodies of RDR supporters.  The RDR reported that 155 of its supporters had been killed during the violence on Oct. 26

Up to 30 people were killed and 200 injured in clashes between supporters of the opposition leader and police and supporters of President Gbagbo in Abidjan, on Dec. 4-5.  The clashes broke out after tens of thousands of Ouattara supporters demonstrated ag

Attempted coup took place on Jan. 7-8 when a group of armed men claiming to be members of the military attacked targets in Abidjan, including the official residence of President Laurent Gbagbo. At least 11 people were killed in the fighting
In early May one supporter died and 30 others were injured during fighting between supporters and police in Abidjan
Fighting broke out early on Sept. 19 in Abidjan, between government forces and rebel soldiers who said that they were protesting against plans to force them to retire
The French reinforced their forces in the country on Oct. 1 and began establishing a tactical command post in Abidjan
Angered by an unconfirmed rumour that de Villepin was about to ask Gbagbo to resign, large crowds of protesters gathered outside the presidential compound in Abidjan, to assail the French minister

ELOCAL: Working class districts, Abobo and Adjame
COMMENT: Serious rioting broke out in Abidjan on Jan. 25 in protest at government concessions made to rebel leaders. Violent rioting, often directed at Westerners and the French in particular, continued in early February
300 people, including many migrants from neighbouring Burkina Faso (which was reportedly financing the rebels) and Mali, had been murdered in Abidjan since the civil war began

A French reporter with Radio France Internationale, was shot dead by a policeman in Abidjan, on Oct. 21.  It was reported that the reporter had been shot as he waited to interview imprisoned opposition figures
Protesters loyal to the government laid siege to France's main army base in Abidjan on Dec. 1-3, prompting French troops to use teargas to try to disperse the crowd
At least 19 people were shot dead in Abidjan on Dec. 12 as gun battles

Fresh violence erupted in Abidjan.  The fighting broke out on March 25 when supporters of the main opposition parties gathered to demonstrate in protest at what they regarded as the government's failure to respect the terms of the Marcoussis peace agreeme
Dozens of Malians had been summarily executed by security forces at a mosque in Abobo, a district of Abidjan, on March 26

France's destruction of the entire Ivorian airforce prompted massive anti-French rioting in Abidjan.  On Nov. 6 tens of thousands of supporters of President Laurent Gbagbo (known as the Young Patriots) took to the streets and congregated around the city a

On Jan. 16 youths supporting Gbagbo-the so-called "Young Patriots"-took to the streets of Abidjan, setting up roadblocks, taking control of many areas of the city, and attacking migrant businesses and shops
Smaller-scale demonstrations occurred in Accra, Colombo, and other African and Asian cities and also in some Western capitals after the murder of Lumumba.

Strike by municipal workers and employees of the trading firm of A. G. Leventis and Co., which is heavily backed by Government loans against the Government's compulsory savings scheme and the price increases on sugar, flour, and other basic commodities, which it was claimed would reduce the workers' living standards below an adequate level. The president called upon all those on strike to return to work "forthwith," declaring that "by 7.30 a.m. on Sept. 19 there should be a full resumption of work throughout the country." protest strike involving 3,000 workers in the petrol and motor industry occurred in Accra on Sept. 18, while the Takoradi strikers ignored the Sept. 19 deadline laid down by Dr. Nkrumah. The strike ended on Sept. 22.

Two bomb explosions in Accra on Nov. 4, the first of which blasted away the feet of the life-size statue of President Nkrumah outside Parliament House, and the second slightly damaged a storeroom at the foot of the large "Freedom and Justice" archway in Black Star Square, where the Queen was to take the salute at a military parade in her honour; a night watchman was injured in the latter explosion.

Three children were killed and a number of persons wounded in Accra on Sept. 9 when a bomb exploded near the President's residence during a demonstration celebrating Dr. Nkrumah's escape from the bomb attack in August. The outrage occurred among a crowd of some 2,000 people dancing in the street 200 yards from the entrance to Flagstaff House (the presidential residence); earlier in the day 20,000 people had taken part in demonstrations against the August attack and in support of the arrest of Mr. Adamafio, Mr. Adjei and Mr. Cofie-Crabbe. The Ghanaian Times alleged on Sept. 10 that the same group was responsible for the latest outrage as for that in August. A Ghana Army warrant officer, Edward Tetteh, committed suicide on Sept. 18 by jumping from a window in the Accra police headquarters, where he was held for questioning over the bomb attacks.

On Sept. 12 some 20,000 people marched to the prison in which Mr. Adamafio and Mr. Adjei were held, demonstrated their support for President Nkrumah, and demanded the execution of the two ex-Ministers.

Two bombs exploded in Accra on Sept. 20 during a torchlight procession held in connexion with celebrations for Dr. Nkrumah's birthday (Sept. 21); seven people were killed and many more injured, the remaining celebrations being immediately cancelled.
A bomb explosion occurred in the centre of Accra on Sept. 22 without, however, causing any casualties.

A bomb exploded in an Accra stadium on Jan. 8, 1963, shortly after the President had left a C.P.P. rally. Strong security forces, including armoured cars, were thrown round Flagstaff Rouse immediately after the attempt.

A gunman unsuccessfully attempted to assassinate President Nkrumah in Accra on Jan. 2. The President was reported to have helped to overpower the gunman, shouting to security officers not to hurt or kill him.
Mass demonstrations took place in Accra, Kumasi, and other centres celebrating the President's escape, demanding "death for the assailants," and calling for stricter security measures.

More than 1,000 people demonstrated on Feb. 4 outside the American Embassy in Accra, carrying placards with slogans such as "Stop your lies and rumours," "You killed Lumumba," and "Down with Yankee imperialism." Police were on duty during the demonstration and the crowd was generally orderly, although one group attempted to haul down the U.S. flag.

Military coup. One of the coup leaders, Brigadier A. K. Ocran, said on March 7 that the action had been carried out by two brigades totalling 3,000 men, and that no more than 27 persons had lost their lives, including seven members of the Army and between 10 and 20 presidential guards at Flagstaff House.

Enthusiastic popular demonstrations in favour of the new regime, and messages of support were received by the National Liberation Council from chiefs. Workers and students demonstrating in Accra, Kumasi, and other centres destroyed pictures of the ex-President; his statue in Accra was overthrown and broken up on Feb. 25.
Coup attempt by army junior officers.
Thousands of mourners demonstrated in Accra to express their support for the National Liberation Council after the officially announce of the death of General Kotoka late on April 17.

Bloodless military coup led by Lieut.-Colonel Ignatius Kutu Acheampong, the acting commander of the 1st Brigade of the Ghana Army. Apart from some shooting near Christiansborg Castle, the seat of the Government, during the night of Jan. 12-13, Accra remained calm.

ACTOR2: The Greater Accra branch of the Ghana Bar Association
COMMENT: The Ghana Medical Association on June 16 called its second strike in a week to demand the reinstatement of the head of the Ghana Medical School and a leading surgeon, whose compulsory retirement as part of the reorganization was described as "an act of victimization". The two men were reinstated on the following day. The Greater Accra branch of the Ghana Bar Association (which had recently called for the early restoration of democratic government in Ghana) on June 17 initiated a strike by lawyers to protest against "the recent dismissals by compulsory retirement of high-ranking officers of state and the manner of these dismissals, including in particular the failure to assign reasons for them". A statement issued by the Bar Association on June 25 called upon the Government to hand over power to a "presidential commission" pending a return to civilian rule.

Lieut.-Colonel George Minyila, a member of the NRC until his appointment as the armed forces' director of logistics in July 1976, had, together with Mr Attoh Quarshie (a businessman and former politician) and a number of other persons, been arrested following an attempt in the previous month to take over Broadcasting House in Accra as part of a plot to overthrow the Acheampong Government.

Incidents in Accra in which "the military's own civilian supporters were roaming in bands through the streets of the capital, intimidating anyone who dared declare his or her opposition to union government".

ELOCAL: British and Nigerian high commissions and the US embassy
COMMENT: Militant students who favoured the continuation of "revolutionary justice" demonstrated in Accra outside the British and Nigerian high commissions and the US embassy (where the American flag was torn down) against "unwarranted interference in the affairs of Ghana by those who have never shown any concern for the suffering of the country and who can have no understanding of the revolutionary process which is unfolding today".

ACTOR1: Supporters of Flt.-Lt. Jerry John Rawlings
COMMENT: Military coup. Some resistance to the coup among Accra-based military units was reported from the Ministry of Defence headquarters, the military training school and the 1st Infantry Brigade, which called for support from the 2nd Infantry Brigade in Takoradi. Resistance by the 1st Infantry Brigade was however overcome by about 6 p.m. on Dec.31, while reinforcements of 2nd Infantry Brigade troops loyal to the Limann Government were defeated about four hours later after being ambushed on the outskirts of Accra. Initial unofficial estimates of casualties during the coup were up to 70 dead and up to 400 wounded, although subsequent official statements implied that the death toll had been between 30 and 40.

Mass rallies and demonstrations which took place in Accra and elsewhere from Jan. 4 onwards in support of the PNDC were well attended, and the considerable personal popularity of Flt.-Lt. Rawlings was apparent to reporters attending rallies at which he spoke.

Coup attempt by a rebel group reportedly acting on orders from Lt.-Col. Ekow Dennis and Capt. Edward Adjei-Ampofo which seized the radio station in Accra. The radio station was recaptured by loyal troops after three hours of fighting during which 26 people, including some of the released detainees, were killed.

A demonstration against the ARPB and in support of the Government was held in Accra on July 30 under the auspices of the 'Organizing Committee for the Defence of the Revolution' (comprising representatives of PDCs, the TUC, workers' defence committees, Prelog and the June 4 Movement). Two masonic lodges were attacked and looted by the demonstrators, and the committee subsequently issued a press release claiming that human skulls had been found in the lodges.

Attempted coup d'etat, reportedly staged by followers of Sgt. Alolga Akata-Pore, a PNDC member, was crushed on Nov. 23 after fighting around PNDC headquarters in Accra and the main army barracks at Burma Camp. A dusk-to-dawn curfew was imposed on Nov. 24, and on Nov. 30 Sgt. Akata-Pore and six other soldiers were arrested 'pending investigations into their role of inciting troops to mutiny'.
At the end of May students demonstrated in Accra and in provincial cities in protest at the composition of the consultative assembly claiming that it was packed with government supporters.

Incidents of violence in different places and four small bomb explosions at locations in Tema and Accra were reported in the ensuing days, and an obscure note sent to the Ghana News Agency indicated that the bombings were part of a campaign to overthrow Rawlings and his ruling Provisional National Defence Council (PNDC). On Nov. 10 a police statement said that "all sensitive install-ations" in the country had been put under 24-hour guard.

Angry demonstrations against government to withdraw value added tax (VAT), which began on May 1 and culminated on May 11, when five people were killed and 17 were injured in clashes between government supporters and about 10,000 marchers making towards Accra's central market.

Assassination of Lt. Vincent Tokfay, a former bodyguard of former Prime Minister in Togo, Joseph Kokou Koffigoh. Tokfay had been forced into exile in Ghana in December 1991 after he had defended the Prime Minister's residence against mutinous troops  in the latest of a series of attacks on the then transitional government. The Togolese government accused him of training armed opposition commandos and had latterly blamed him for a number of cross-border raids, including a failed attack on President Gnassingbe Eyadema in March 1993. The Ghanaian authorities said that two Togolese agents were suspected of his murder, a view shared by the Togolese opposition Union of the Forces of Change.

The NPP member of parliament for the Ablekuma district of the capital city, Accra, was killed when he attempted to prevent security personnel from destroying a ballot box.  However, most analysts agreed that the attempts at intimidation by NDC supporters had failed and that the overall validity of the poll had not been compromised by such incidents.

A section of the Ethiopian Imperial Guard and the police carried out a coup d'etat in Addis Ababa on Dec. 14. The revolt was crashed by Dec. 17, when the Emperor returned to the capital. Fighting continued for some days on the outskirts of the capital and in the surrounding hills, all the main rebel leaders being killed or captured. Casualties during the fighting were announced on Dec. 20 as follows: armed forces--29 killed, 43 wounded; Imperial Guard--174 killed, 300 wounded; civilians--121 killed, 442 wounded. Seven foreigners were known to have been killed, including a British subject from Aden.

Some 1,000 demonstrators stormed into the grounds of the British Embassy in Addis Ababa on Nov. 12, breaking windows and causing much damage in protest against Rhodesia's U.D.I. Placards were carried bearing such slogans as "Smith --dead or alive," "Down with the colonialists," and "Down with Bands" (the Prime Minister of Malawi).

Explosion in a cinema on Nov. 19, in which 36 persons, including a number of non-Ethiopians, were injured, the police announced on Nov. 28 the arrest of a lieutenant charged with having thrown grenades into the cinema, as well as that of several other persons, including General Tadesse Biru, accused of conspiracy against the Government.

Student demonstrations on April 11-15 against a new law, approved by Parliament and making the assembly of more than five persons subject to previous permission, were dispersed by the police using tear-gas. A police spokesman Stated that 20 students had been arrested, but the students gave the number of arrests as 120 and alleged that numerous students had been injured by the police.

In early April 1968 violent clashes between students and police occurred in Addis Ababa over a period of several days, the immediate cause being a rumour that students who had taken part in an earlier demonstration were to be expelled from the University and that their association would be banned.

Further disturbances in March and April 1969 led to led to the arrest and detention for 24 hours of some 500 students on April 3 and the closure of the Haile Selassie I University and all secondary schools from March 3-10.

Closure by the Government of all secondary schools in the capital following a clash between police and secondary school pupils on April 23 in which one student was killed and two others seriously injured.
Assasination of Mr. Gizaw Tilahun, the president of the students' union, who had been killed by shots fired from a passing car.
Security forces fired on large crowds of students who had refused to surrender the body of Mr. Gizaw Tilahun, the president of the students' union, which had been assasinated the day before.

Student demonstration in which bus services in the chopping district of Addis Ababa were stopped took place on May 20, 1971, following a decision by the University authorities that a newly formed students' union was illegal.

3,500 taxi and bus drivers struck in protest against increased licence fees and petrol prices. A teachers' strike took place at the same time in support of demands for educational reforms and higher pay, in view of the rising cost of living. The Emperor said that three persons had been killed (though unofficial reports mentioned up to nine people), 22 injured and nearly 1,000 arrested.

Early on Feb. 28 units of the Fourth Division of the Army took control of Addis Ababa. While protesting their loyalty to the Emperor, they set up road blocks, occupied Radio Ethiopia and the airport, sealed off the National Bank, and imposed a curfew. Leaflets were distributed from aircraft containing a number of demands, among them a call for the establishment of a special court to investigate alleged corruption among Cabinet Ministers.

Militant students demonstrated in the streets of the capital, beginning on March 1, in favour of land reform, free elections for a democratic Parliament and freedom of expression; on some posters the dismal of the now Prime Minister was also demanded. The demonstrations were, however, dispersed by the police who fired several shots, wounding a number of students on March 1, one of whom died later.

General strike called by the Confederation of Labour Unions which demanded free elections, a free Press, all-round pay increases to a minimum basic wage of three Ethiopian dollars and the right to strike for workers in the public sector. Late on March 8 agreement was reached "in principle" between trade union and government representatives to end the general strike.

Demands for higher pay and increased benefits were also made on March 11 by representatives of the 200,000 priests of the Orthodox Church and on March 14 by tobacco workers, who went on strike together with other groups of workers.

Further anti-Government demonstration by some 1,000 university students was forcibly dispersed by police on March 11, and on March 13 the police arrested eight university teachers for distributing "subversive" leaflets.
On March 17 some 5,000 women demonstrated--for the first time in Ethiopia--for equal pay and rights.
Some 6,000 strikers demonstrating in the streets enforced the dismissal of the Mayor and other senior municipal officers on April 11
Some 30,000 Moslems demonstrated in Addis Ababa in favour of equal rights for Moslems and Christians and the separation of Church and State.
Counter-demonstrations by Christians and they petitioned the Patriarch Theophilos and the Prime Minister to use their influence to keep Ethiopia "a Christian country"
The police opened fire on demonstrating strikers and students in Addis Ababa on April 25, but no casualties were reported.

Police and troop units led began to round up former Ministers, military officers and civilian officials, some of whom they suspected of trying to overthrow the Government. Armed forces units also took action against strikers when they forcibly ended a telecommunication workers' strike on April 30 and a postal workers' strike on May 3. Army units on June 28 occupied all government and private radio stations, as well as the telecommunications centre and the airport, and ordered the broadcasting of their own communiqués. On July 2 armed forces units occupied the banks; according to press reports, no clients were permitted to withdraw more than 10,000 Ethiopian dollars (about Ł2,000) from their accounts.
Demonstration against the Emperor Haile Sellassie, took place on Sept. 2, but further such demonstrations were banned by the Armed Forces Committee.
A second Addis Ababa demonstration, in which protesters chanted "Hang the Emperor", was quickly dispersed by troops on Sept. 8.

The Emperor Haile Selassie (82) was deposed on Sept. 12 at a formal ceremony conducted at his palace by the Armed Forces Committee which in a proclamation read out to him, said that they were deposing him because of alleged corruption and neglect, details of which had previously been made public, and that a provisional Military Government would be established. Haile Selassie was subsequently taken away from the palace, by a heavily armed escort, to a "place of safety", believed to be the headquarters of the Army's Fourth Division, where some 160 persons, including members of the royal family and former court and government officials, were being held pending their trial for various offenses.
Several thousand demonstrating students who on Sept. 16 called for a "civilian people's Government". Their demonstration was, however, dispersed by troops and riot police.

The Grand Palace in Addis Ababa was partly demolished by a tank and by mortar fire. It was believed that General Aman Andom, together with some of his supporters, had been killed while resisting arrest, and this was confirmed on Nov.26 by the Military Government, which added that it had ordered the execution of the other former officials because they had been" found guilty of maladministration, hindering fair administration of justice, selling secret documents of the country to foreign agents and attempting to disrupt the present Ethiopian popular movement".

ACTOR1: Followers of the deposed feudal nobility
COMMENT: Bombs exploded, causing an unknown number of casualties, at a fuel depot at 'the Bole international airport in Addis Ababa (late on Nov.30); at the Wabe Shebelle hotel, and the city hall in Addis Ababa (on Dec. 2); and at the tobacco monopoly building (on Dec. 3). The explosions were officially attributed to followers of the deposed feudal nobility and to relatives of those executed on Nov.23, but independent observers said that they might be the work of members of the Eritrean Liberation Front.

Ethiopian Airlines employees attempted on Sept.25 to distribute at Addis Ababa airport leaflets officially described as "hostile to the revolution", security forces tried to arrest one of the employees; after hundreds of demonstrators had begun to obstruct the former, firing broke out and the security forces killed four and wounded 19 persons, three of whom died later.
Strike by teachers. Some 40 teachers were arrested in Addis Ababa for taking part in the strike which was called by their union.

Major-General Kebede Worku, former commander of the Imperial Guard, and Kegnazmatch Makounen Wossen, a prominent landlord, were shot dead at their homes in Addis Ababa by security forces, allegedly as they were resisting arrest.
200 actors and stage workers demonstrated in Addis Ababa in favour of forming a union on April 22, police killed one of them, wounded another and arrested about 70 persons.
The May Day celebrations were disturbed by anti-government groups who clashed with security forces, one civilian being killed and two others wounded.

During a meeting held in Addis Ababa early in June to discuss the formation of an overall union of industrial workers from nearly 240 industrial establishments, five persons were arrested because they were said to have infiltrated the meeting as "counter-revolutionary agents”.
Brigadier-General Getachew Nadew was killed by security forces after he had fired at a soldier bringing him a message from the PMAC.
Assasination of Mr Fikre Merid, a French-educated member of the "secretariat for the organization of the people"

Some 200 kebele (neighbourhood association) office-bearers had been assassinated in Addis Ababa alone by members of various Marxist factions, and another 200 seriously injured, during the first nine months of 1977.

Brigadier-General Teferi Benti, the Ethiopian head of state, and a number of his colleagues on the ruling military council was killed by other members of this council in a battle at the Grand Palace in Addis Ababa after "a few counter-revolutionaries and reactionaries" had "attempted to overthrow the revolution" by means of a "fascist coup d'état". The radio stated that Brigadier-General Teferi Benti and eight other men had been "executed" for being variously members of an underground Marxist group, a right-wing faction or an Eritrean secessionist movement.

Mr Vondima Destu, head of Addis Ababa's department of planning was assassinated on Feb. 13. The principal organization held responsible for such murders was the illegal (Marxist-Leninist) Ethiopian People's Revolutionary Party (EPRPage 28421), which was opposed to the regime on the grounds that a military junta would not be able to achieve a genuine revolution.
Ato Tewodros Bekele, leader of the newly-formed All-Ethiopian Trade Union, who was killed in his office on Feb. 24 after receiving death threats from the EPRP
Mr William Hastings Morton, a British university lecturer, was shot dead by gunmen in Addis Ababa on March 10
The Ethiopian head of state repeated his charges at a mass rally in Addis Ababa on April 14, when he exhorted Ethiopians "to get armed" in order to fight the Sudan as well as the EDU and the ELF
On April 29-May 1 between 300 and 500 students and other young people accused of distributing anti-Government leaflets were reported to have been killed

Diplomatic sources in Addis Ababa, quoted on Oct.13, stated that up to 350 persons had been killed in political violence in the capital during the previous two weeks after demonstrations by anti- government left-wing students protesting against alleged executions carried out in prisons.
Lieutenant Gizew Temesgen, a member of the PMAC and head of the information and communications section of its standing committee, was murdered. "Hired assassins" were held responsible.
Lieutenant Solomon Legesse, a member of the Revolutionary Defence Committee, was killed on Nov. 13. "Hired assassins" were held responsible

Assasination of Mr Worky Tegegn, head of the Addis Ababa office of the Ethiopian news agency. The principal organization held responsible for such murders was the illegal (Marxist-Leninist) Ethiopian People's Revolutionary Party (EPRPage 28421), which was opposed to the regime on the grounds that a military junta would not be able to achieve a genuine revolution.

Security forces moved at plotters which was attemting a coup senior generals. Presidential guards and tank units then surrounded the Ministry, and the Defence Minister, Maj.- Gen. Haile Giorgis Habte Mariam, went in to dissuade the plotters, whereupon he was himself shot dead, allegedly by the Industry Minister and former Air Force Commander Fanta Belai) (who was later arrested). Several hundred people were killed or arrested on May 16-17 in Addis Ababa as security forces, paratroops and tank units eliminated remaining resistance, notably at the Ground Forces headquarters. 27 senior army officers were among rebels killed during the events of May 16-18.
One of the alleged ringleaders of the coup attempt in May, Maj.-Gen. Aberra Abebe, former chief of operations at the Defence Ministry, was killed during a shoot-out in Addis Ababa on June 29.

Addressing a crowd of 25,000 in the capital Addis Ababa on Sept. 12, President Mengistu Haile Mariam appealed to Ethiopians to defend their country before it reached a point of "collapse and no return". Mengistu said that it was "imperative" that the current civil war be brought to a "definite end with the minimum of delay", and he accused the EPLF and the Tigrean People's Liberation Front (TPLF) of receiving "unlimited" foreign assistance to destroy Ethiopia.
A number of residents of the Shagolle suburb had been killed on May 28 when a shell hit a munitions plant

Up to seven people were killed when EPRDF forces opened fire on demonstrators in Addis Ababa on May 29. Crowds of 100 to 400 people were reported to have gathered in various parts of the city, chanting anti-EPRDF, anti-US and pro-Ethiopian unity slogans. Demonstrations were declared illegal, but continued over the next two days.
Five people, including two EPRDF guards, were killed by gunmen at Addis Ababa University on May 31.

Over 100 people were estimated to have been killed, and about 7,000 made homeless, by a series of explosions at an ammunition dump in the southern Addis Ababa suburb of Bekulo Bet on June 4. The EPRDF attributed the disaster to pro-Mengistu saboteurs; local residents said that a gun-battle had preceded the explosions.

At least one person was killed and 30 were wounded, 10 seriously, when about 1,000 student protesters clashed with security forces in Addis Ababa on Jan. 4, during a visit by Boutros Boutros-Ghali, the UN Secretary-General. The students were said to be planning to march to a conference centre where Boutros-Ghali was to open a Somali peace conference, to protest against his intended visit to Eritrea two days later, and against the UN's intention to send observers to the Eritrean independence referendum in April. The students also reportedly intended to protest against the ruling Ethiopian People's Revolutionary Democratic Front (EPRDF) government's readiness to allow the Eritrean independence process to take its course.

Seven officials of opposition parties were reported to have been arrested in Addis Ababa in the days before a conference on peace and reconciliation organized there by some 50 opposition groups on Dec. 18-22. A government statement on Dec. 22 said that those arrested would be tried, and that they were either "officials of the former regime" or belonged to organizations currently carrying on "terrorist operations".

Some 7,000 people demonstrated in Addis Ababa on Feb. 10, calling for seven identified suspects to be tried for taking part in the murder of an estimated 55,000 people in Addis Ababa alone during 1978-79.

ACTOR1: Members of the dissolved Supreme Council for Islamic Affairs
TARGET1: Committee Organizing Islamic Affairs in Ethiopia
COMMENT: At least 11 people were reported killed and more than 150 others injured in clashes between rival Muslim factions on Feb. 21. Some 50 people were arrested.

President Mohammed Hosni Mubarak of Egypt was the target of an assassination attempt on the morning of June 26. Gunmen emerged from a rented suburban villa and opened fire on the motorcade taking him from the airport to the Organization of African Unity (OAU) summit meeting. Several bullets hit Mubarak's car, and two Ethiopian policemen were killed and another wounded, before Mubarak's guards succeeded in driving off the assailants, two of whom were killed.
On July 1 police killed three suspects from the assassination attempt on the president of Egypt, in a raid on a hideout in Addis Ababa.
Bomb explosion in an Addis Ababa hotel on Aug. 5, which killed one person.

A series of grenade attacks. The most serious incident was on April 11 when two grenades were thrown at diners at the Bleu Tops restaurant, killing a waitress and injuring 41 people. In the third attack in three days, a grenade was thrown into a crowded supermarket on April 13, injuring 33 people. No group took responsibility for the attacks. Responsibility for similar attacks in 1996 had been claimed by Al-Itihad Al-Islam, a group of ethnic Somalis fighting for the independence of Ogaden.

TARGET1: Remnants of the rebel Oromo Liberation Fron
COMMENT: Three men, allegedly carrying illegal weapons, were killed and two others injured in an exchange of fire with police in Addis Ababa on Oct. 9. According to Radio Ethiopia, the men were the "remnants" of the rebel Oromo Liberation Front and had been responsible for an attack on a village in Hamle in July.

On April 17-18 police in Addis Ababa (the capital) clashed with students who had attacked the offices of the National Examination Centre during a demonstration over academic rights.  Serious rioting spread throughout the streets of the capital killing some 31 people and injuring 250 others. Security forces opened fire on the students who had attacked state-owned buildings and looted the city’s commercial districts. Coup attempt by The Prime Minister of Senegal, M. Mamadou Dia. During the night of Dec. 16-17 some territorial guard units were ordered into the capital by M. Dia, and on the following morning these troops, together with gendarmerie, took possession of the Parliament building, Dakar radio, and the Government headquarters. By the night of Dec. 17-18 the forces loyal to President Senghor were in full control of Dakar; the four deputies who had been arrested under M. Dia's orders were released; and the gendarmerie and territorial guards, together with the Dakar police force, also declared their loyalty to the President.

The students' union at the University of Dakar on May 18, 1968, called for a strike to begin on May 27 in protest against a 22 per cent reduction in certain grants. On May 29 the police were ordered to expel students from the University buildings they had occupied, at least one student being killed and many injured in the process. The Government decided the same day to close the University and secondary schools for an indefinite period; all places of entertainment in Dakar were also closed, and the assembly of more than five persons and all meetings were prohibited. The Union nationale des travailleurs du Senegal (UNTS) thereupon called an unlimited strike to protest against the "violation of personal and democratic freedom".A state of emergency was declared on May 31. On June 2, 36 persons were sentenced to imprisonment from 18 months to three years for damage or injuries caused in demonstrations, but 31 trade unionists and five women demonstrators, arrested on May 31 and June 1, were released on June 8.

Counter-demonstrations had been staged in Dakar on June 3 by peasants from the countryside expressing their loyalty to the President. Dakar subsequently returned to normal except for an incident at Pikine (a suburb of the city) on June 14, when a young man was killed while police were dispersing demonstrators.

Secondary school pupils' strike was supported by the students' union. The strikes nevertheless continued, and the President claimed on April 3 that behind the agitation there was "an attempt at subversion directed from abroad". When strikers attempted on April 21 to evict non-strikers from classrooms, the police intervened with tear-gas and made several arrests, while on May 6 police dispersed pickets from the University and cleared the buildings of strikers. By this time pupils had begun gradually to return to school.

10-day strike was called by bank officials. As under the country's labour legislation the strike was illegal, the Government authorized the employers to dismiss strikers and to recruit new staff. Negotiations between employers and strikers failed to end in agreement, and the UNTS decided to call a 48-hour general strike on June 11. President Senghor thereupon declared a state of emergency. The strike, however, was a failure and led to a split in the trade union movement
Malian students occupied the Malian embassy in Dakar (Senegal) for several hours on March 24 to protest over the death of Mr Camara and to demand the release of other detainees in Mali.

Students at the University of Dakar boycotted classes between March and May 1984, presenting a list of grievances to the authorities including demands for increased grants and better transport and other facilities, and expressing anxiety about the country's economic situation. In late April President Diouf ordered troops to seal off the campus and appealed to students to return to work. The government acceded to several of the students' demands in mid-May and the situation returned to normal.

Violence broke out in the centre of Dakar in July when rival factions in the Confederation nationale des travailleurs senegalais (CNTS)-affiliated to the PS-clashed at the union's headquarters. Members of a minority faction opposed to the confederation's secretary-general, Mr Madia Diop, were apparently attacked while attempting to hold a meeting. At least one person was killed, while one report put the number of dead at six, with between 50 and 70 people injured. A strike by transport workers, who had been involved in the fighting, later took place.

The secretary-general of the PDS, Mr Abdoulaye Wade was arrested in Dakar (the capital) along with some 11 other PDS members on April 16, 1985, on charges of having held an unauthorized demonstration; Mr Wade had on the previous day called upon his supporters to attend meetings at mosques and churches in order to pray for 'radical political change' in the country. Those arrested were released on April 24.

Demonstration against apartheid in South Africa, called by ADS. Mr Wade and Mr Bathily were among some 15 people arrested on Aug. 23 on the grounds that they had participated in a demonstration which had been banned by the governor of Dakar.

The government in February 1987 had agreed upon improvements in the payment of grants and in students' standards of living following a 32-day strike by students at the Cheikh Anta Diop University in Dakar.

The entire police force was suspended after some of its members staged an illegal strike on April 13-14, 1987. Under the terms of the 1963 Constitution for approval of which , police officers were prohibited from striking. The immediate motive for the police action was the sentencing (to two-year prison terms) on April 10 of seven police officers who had been found guilty of torturing to death a detainee in 1983. Policemen were reportedly disgruntled that the commanding officer at the site of the incident was not charged; dissatisfaction with pay and conditions was also considered to be an influence in the police action. On the second day of the strike, which had been called in defiance of government warnings, scuffles broke out between police officers and gendarmes in the centre of Dakar.

Violence flared up in the streets of Dakar when preliminary election results showed a large majority for President Diouf and the PS. The government responded by declaring a state of emergency in the Dakar region on Feb. 29 and imposed a dusk till dawn curfew; some 400 soldiers were deployed in the capital. Schools and the university, from where Mr Wade drew much of his support, were closed following clashes between police and students, in which 13 students were arrested. The authorities denied reports that two people had been killed in the unrest. The National Assembly on March 11 approved a request from President Diouf for an indefinite extension of the state of emergency.

Rioting broke out in Dakar on April 21-25, with thousands of Mauritanians taking refuge in their country's embassy and in the main mosque. A curfew was imposed in Dakar and a national state of emergency declared on April 28. Estimates of the numbers of people killed, contested by both governments, were most commonly given at around 400-450, the majority of them apparently in Mauritania.

Three-day strike strike by an alliance of trade unions against wage cuts. The strike was only partially observed in Dakar, while a call for similar action on Oct. 26 appeared by have had little affect.

Demonstrators marched towards the presidential palace calling for the overthrow of President Abdou Diouf. The government on Feb. 17 proscribed a radical Islamic movement, Moustarchidine Oua Moustarchidate (Men and Women Fighting for Truth, also known as "Guides"). The ban came 24 hours after fighting in Dakar between security forces and thousands of armed demonstrators, in which five policemen and one civilian were killed.
Violent demonstrations organized by opposition parties in protest at the devaluation. Six people were killed.

Crowds of distressed relatives had demonstrated outside the presidential palace in Dakar, demanding to know why the ferry, designed to carry 550 passengers, had been so overloaded, after an overcrowded ocean ferry sailing north from Ziguinchor to the capital, Dakar, capsized in a storm during the early hours of Sept. 27, killing almost 1,000 people.

Army mutineers made their way into Dar-es-Salaam, where shooting took place in the streets, shops were looted by Africans, the post office was surrounded by rebels, and the airport was closed. Mr. Stephen Miles (the Acting British High Commissioner) and Mr. Job Lusinde (the Minister of Home Affairs) were arrested by mutineers, but were later released; in other incidents mutineers and uniformed members of the youth league of the Tanganyika African National Union (TANU) threatened and manhandled a number of Europeans, including a Czechoslovak diplomat. According to a Commonwealth Relations Office statement, 14 persons (all of them non-Europeans) were killed and about 120 injured in the riots.
Mass demonstration against the British government in connection with disorders in southern Rhodesia.

Armed attack on Frelimo's offices in Dar-es-Salaam took place on May 13, 1968, one member of its central committee being killed and several of its supporters injured. Protest demonstrations against the arrests of Three leading members of the National Democratic Party (N.D.P.), began in Salisbury's African townships of Harari and Highfield before dawn on July 20, about half the city's African workers failing to report for work. The majority of Africans returned to work on July 21, but outbursts of rioting continued throughout the day, cars being stoned and Africans going to work being beaten up. The police repeatedly used tear-gas to disperse rioters and made numerous arrests.

Rioting in Salisbury on Oct. 8 followed an incident in which an African eyelist was knocked down and killed by a European motorist in Harare township; riot police were summoned and used tear-gas to scatter the crowd which had assembled. A large numbers of migrant workers came out of the nearby hostels, looted and set fire to European-owned shops and cafes and to a beer-hall, and stoned cars and buses containing Europeans; throwing stones and bottles, the rioters kept up a running fight for eight hours with the police, who used riot guns and later sub-machine guns. Seven Africans were shot dead; about 70 were treated for shot wounds and about 100 for other wounds; and over 100 were arrested. Twenty Europeans were injured by stones.

Police patrols opened fire after their vehicles had been stoned in Highfield township, following sporadic incidents earlier in the day--including stoning and intimidation--which had led to the arrest of eight Africans. Road blocks were set up round Highfields and other African townships, and municipal buses travelling through the townships were given police protection after stoning incidents. One African was killed and five wounded. N.D.P. disclaimed all responsibility for the incidents at Highfields

Demonstrations against the new Constitution. The first day of the demonstrations, which were carried out initially by African women, passed off quietly, although many women were arrested. During the afternoon African men began demonstrating outside the Salisbury central police station and in Harare and Highfield townships. Hundreds gathered outside the police station, the police opening fire after the crowd had begun to stone them; later the police also opened fire against rioters in Harare township, killing one African. Some demonstrations continued on Dec. 8, but without serious incidents.

ACTOR1: Southern Rhodesian African Trade Union Congress
COMMENT: 24-hour strike called on May 14 by the Southern Rhodesian African Trade Union Congress in protest against "working conditions and lack of freedom of speech and assembly." Many Africans who defied the strike call were attacked by intimidators, a number being badly injured; three demonstrators were killed in clashes with the police; and some 30 arrests were made.
Police raid in Harare township against ZAPU. The police seized 700 home-made weapons.

Disturbances broke out in Salisbury on Jan. 16, 1964, when a crowd had gathered to greet Mr. Nkomo, leading to arson and looting in the African township of Harare and to the arrest of more than 30 persons. Stoning of houses and individuals, assaults. and intimidation continued on subsequent days, and by Jan. 26 another 45 people had been arrested. Two days later a crowd protesting against the conviction of Mr. Nkomo at Umtali attacked buses, cars, and individuals in Salisbury; when the police opened fire two Africans were killed and a third was wounded, while many others were injured by gangs of violent Africans. By Jan. 29 some 80 Africans had been arrested for acts of violence, but an attempt by PCC supporters to organize a general strike failed.

Demonstrations took place in Salisbury on April 17 against Mr. Nkomo's removal to the restriction area, the police arresting 120 women, of whom 105 were sentenced on April 28 to Ł20 or three months' imprisonment, and three to Ł25 or three months. Further demonstrations occurred on April 18 when between 20 and 30 Africans assaulted White women and children in a Salisbury supermarket.
A PCC official was killed at Harare on June 13
An African died of burns on June 18 after a gang had attacked and set fire to his house in Highfield.
A ZANU official released from the Wha Wha restriction area was battered to death in Highfield.

Gangs tearing down the numbers of houses in the township so that postal deliveries were suspended; this appeared to be largely the work of an extremist group hostile to both the PCC and ZANU.
Killing of african in Harare for political reasons.
Several incidents involving the throwing of bombs in Salisbury on Nov. 8-Dec. 1.

More than 5,000 Africans demonstrating for the release of Mr. Nkomo (the detained leader of the banned People's Caretaker Council) and for a system of "one man, one vote", when the two British Ministers arrived in Salisbury on Feb. 21.

ELOCAL: Outside the house of the Principal
COMMENT: Sit-down demonstration on March 16 outside the house of the Principal, Dr. Walter Adams, calling for the latter's resignation and a more positive stand at the University against the Smith Government's policies. They also complained about new regulations giving the Students' Representative Council fresh disciplinary powers over students. As demonstrations continued on March 18, police entered the College campus and ordered the demonstrators to disperse. As a result 20 lecturers ceased work, while most arts and social studies students boycotted the remaining lectures. The police ban was relaxed on March 21, so that in effect all meetings except demonstrations were again permitted.
On June 13 stoning and arson in an African township caused damage estimated at Ł50,000 to a furniture warehouse.

During the second round of talks of the informal talks between officials of the British Government and of the Smith regime, Mr. Watson, Mr. Le Quesne, Mr. Neale and Mr. Hennings were on July 1 forced by about 60 hostile African students to leave the grounds of the University College, to which they had been invited.

More than 50 students demonstrated against the visit of Dr. J. P. Duminy, vice-chancellor of the University of Cape Town, and prevented two Rhodesian Ministers, Mr. A. P. Smith (Education) and Mr. J. J. Wrathall (Finance), from attending. White students thereupon called for the expulsion of the demonstrators.

On Aug. 12 a hand-grenade was thrown into a cafe and injured seven White people; four Africans were subsequently arrested and committed for trial. In Salisbury two Africans were sentenced to death on Sept. 21 for their part in the hand-grenade attack on a cafe in August, while a third was found not guilty and discharged; one of those sentenced to death, described by the judge as "the ringleader of the gang," declared that the deed had been committed as "a protest against U.D.I. and the Smith regime."
A bomb was thrown into a flat on Aug. 23 without injuring anyone.
A petrol bomb thrown by Africans in Salisbury on Sept. 16 caused a fire destroying a building containing four shops.

Rioting occurred at the African township of Harare, on Jan. 19, as gangs of youths rampaged through the streets. Four people were killed. It was difficult to pinpoint the exact cause of the disorder, but there was no question of it being directed against the Commission when Africans were shot dead by the police and 44 arrested, including 24 wounded by gunshots.
Hostile demonstrations by several hundred African secondary school pupils on Aug. 2 in reaction to the legislations decision that  Africans could, for the first time, be called up for armed service.

About 150 Africans demonstrated on Aug. 3 against alleged low wages of African workers employed by the University and against racial discrimination in staff appointments; when the students began to damage property, the Principal, Professor Robert Craig, had called for police protection. Following a recommendation by a disciplinary committee that six students should be expelled and another eight suspended for their activities during the demonstration of Aug. 3.
Rioting Black students stoned buildings on Aug. 7, the police arresting 165 of them.

Clashes took place between ZANU supporters, who demonstrated for chimurenga ("liberation war") and the expulsion of Whites from Rhodesia, and ZAPU supporters who opposed them and assaulted ZANU members of the committee. The two groups were separated by armed White policemen with dogs and, as a crowd of ZANU supporters advanced against the police and threw stones and bricks at them, the police opened fire, killing five men within a few minutes.
Two Soviet-made hand grenades were thrown at the bishop's house in Salisbury without causing injury to anyone.

A meeting called to hold "a properly constituted annual congress of the ANC" turned into a mass rally of support for The Muzorewa wing at Highfield, Salisbury, on Oct.26, attended by more than 35,000 people, and this turnout was seen as corroborating a claim by Dr Elliott Gabellah, the wing's deputy president, that it enjoyed the support of more than 80 per cent of Rhodesia's Africans.

Bishop Muzorewa returned to Rhodesia on July 17 from a six-week absence in search of international support for his movement and was received by over 100,000 supporters in Salisbury, where he announced a new programme of "action" and subsequently made further statements concerning a constitutional settlement.

Bomb explosion on Aug. 6 in a Woolworth store in Salisbury, where 11 persons-eight Blacks and three Whites-were killed and 76 other persons injured. A ZANU official in Lusaka was later reported as stating that this bomb was "the Patriotic Front's answer to new negotiations" and that Zimbabwe would be "liberated by the gun and the bomb".
Mr Simon Chengeta, a Black member of the House of Assembly, had been kidnapped and killed on March 19 when "terrorists" had forced his son to beat him to death.

The last of six guerrillas sent to Rhodesia to assassinate black leaders had been killed by police in a shoot-out in Highfield (Salisbury), in which three other persons, including a police driver, had also lost their lives.

Bomb explosion in Salisbury on April 25, when a man died who was thought to have been trying to set the timing device of the bomb.

Armed men attacked the home (in an inner White suburb of Salisbury) of Archbishop Kyrillos Papadopoulos, the head of the Greek Orthodox Church in Zimbabwe/Rhodesia. No one was injured in the raid, and the attackers afterwards evaded a police pursuit. Reports suggested that the probable intended target had been the nearby home of Lt.-Gen. Walls.
Demonstration by ZAPU supporters in Salisbury on Dec. 13 (which was peacefully dispersed after a police warning). On Dec. 14 the ZAPU offices in Salisbury were sealed off after a police raid.

Mr Oliver Saunyama, a candidate for Mr Sithole's ZANU, was shot dead in his car leaving his home in Salisbury on Jan.22, after which Mr Sithole alleged that the attack had been part of a campaign by ZANU (PF) to eliminate political opponents
Attacks on political candidate included an attempt to assassinate Mr Mugabe on Feb. 5; a grenade attack on his home in Salisbury, Mr Mugabe alleged on Feb. 6 that the SFAs were responsible.
The Salisbury home of Mr Ernest Bulle (UANC) was attacked on Feb. 14.

Bomb attacks on two churches in Salisbury on Feb.14, and the discovery of an unexploded bomb in the Roman Catholic cathedral in the city the following day, it was reportedly initially suspected that the bombs were part of an alleged ZANU (PF) anti-religious campaign (the cathedral bomb having been contained in a briefcase marked with party slogans). Mr Mugabe denied that his party was involved (as did Mr Nkomo), and Mr Justin Nyoka, a spokesman for ZANU (PF), asserted on Feb.15 that the Rhodesian security forces were using "dirty tricks" in a crude attempt to discredit the party.

Two men, suspected by police of having placed the church bombs, which were set off by timing devices, killed by a bomb explosion in their car on Feb. 14. They were were identified as Rhodesian security force personnel. Whereas the communiqué indicated that the men had been acting on their own initiative, Mr Mugabe on Feb.18 alleged that the incident was "part of an ill-conceived plot to besmirch ZANU (PF)", intended to encourage the false belief that the party was anti-religious; the Governor's office issued no statement except to say that the matter was under investigation.

Mr Joe Gqabi, a senior member of the ANC executive and ANC representative in Zimbabwe, was assassinated outside his Salisbury home on July 31, 1981. Dr Shamuyarira blamed South African agents for the killing and confirmed his Government's support for the guerrilla war being waged in South Africa, whereas official South African sources claimed that the killing was the result of factional rivalry within the ranks of the ANC.

ACTOR2: Elements of Zimbabwe's Army and police force
COMMENT: A bomb blast occurred at ZANU(PF)'s party headquarters in Salisbury on Dec.18, 1981, which resulted in seven deaths (at an adjoining bakery) and many injuries. The likely target of the attack was a ZANU(PF) central committee meeting which was due to be held that afternoon. The Zimbabwean media immediately accused South Africa of responsibility for the attack, but on Dec, 27 Mr Mugabe blamed elements of Zimbabwe's Army and police force.
Mr Mugabe's decision to dismiss Mr Nkomo from the Government was supported by peaceful demonstrations in many towns including Salisbury
Clashes occurred between rival supporters in the Salisbury township of St Mary's on March 21.

ELOCAL: Houses of Mr Mugabe and Mr Nkala in Harare
COMMENT: The Harare homes of Mr Mugabe and Mr Nkala were attacked on June 24, 1982, by several gunman, one of whom was killed at Mr Nkala's house. Eight soldiers and an undisclosed number of civilians were detained in connexion with these attacks and were later identified by government sources as former ZIPRA guerrillas. Mr Mugabe on July 3 linked the ZAPU leader with these attacks.
Mr Mugabe told some 500 women at a demonstration in Harare on May 4 that a shake-up from top to bottom in the country's leadership was necessary for Zimbabwe to achieve its socialist objectives.

Demonstrations by ZANU supporters against followers of Mr Nkomo in the Midlands towns of Gweru, Kadoma, Kwekwe and Norton and also in Harare from June 14 to June 26 led to violent incidents in which 10 people were killed and more than 300 injured; the ZAPU office in Gweru was burned down and all the party's significant offices outside Bulawayo and Harare were closed.

A wave of reprisals against supporters of ZAPU and the UANC took place on July 7-9 in Harare and other towns, which left six people dead according to unconfirmed reports. Several hundred homes were reported to have been ransacked and their owners evicted in Harare, in Bindura and Glendale (both north of the capital) and in the Midlands town of Kwekwe.

ELOCAL: Home of Mr Nkomo's Bulawayo in Harare
COMMENT: The police on July 23 confiscated a small armoury of weapons from Mr Nkomo's Bulawayo home, and security officers took into custody 11 men, mostly Mr Nkomo's guards, from his Harare home. A police spokesman stated that the weapons had been taken for analysis in connexion with a series of incidents. Mr Munangagwa, speaking on the same day in the House of Assembly, revealed that a dissident had been arrested at Mr Nkomo's house, but the ZAPU leader alleged that he was himself being 'set up' for assassination.
The Harare magistrates' court was damaged by fire on Nov. 18 in an attack in which the walls were also sprayed with anti-government slogans.

Mrs Tsiti Chiliza, who was believed to have been married to a South African exile, was killed on May 11 by a television bomb in Harare. Mr Mugabe described the attack, which was believed to have been aimed at an unnamed senior official of the African National Congress (ANC, banned in South Africa), as part of 'South Africa's iniquitous policy aimed at destabilizing the front-line states'.

The University of Harare (the country's only university) was closed on Oct. 4 after clashes between police and students protesting against the arrest of two student leaders who had attacked the government as "neo-fascist", corrupt and reactionary.

Class boycotts and demonstrations in May had frequently degenerated into violence as students clashed with the police. During one demonstration in Harare in late May students reportedly caused damage estimated at Z$500,000 (nearly US$100,000). On June 6 the University of Zimbabwe authorities took action against students protesting at the high level of fees and demanding higher grants. All 10,000 students were expelled.

On June 13, riot police were deployed to prevent a series of nationwide demonstrations which had been banned by the government on June 10. The demonstrations had been planned by the Zimbabwe Congress of Trade Unions (ZCTU) as part of its campaign for the suspension of the government's economic reform programme and the repeal of new labour legislation. Police broke up a small gathering in Harare and arrested at least six trade unionists.
National strike public-sector workers called by Public Services Association (PSA).

The country's health service was said to be in crisis at the end of November as a dispute by nursing staff and doctors remained unsettled. The strike had begun in October in protest at the non-payment of increased allowances promised after the civil servants' strike in August. The government refused to negotiate and dismissed all strikers; there were also reports of the arrest of union leaders who had returned to work. By Nov. 25 all the main hospitals were effectively closed.

Rioting broke out in the centre and outskirts of the capital, Harare, on Jan. 19, following the introduction on that day of a 21 percent increase in the retail price of maize meal, Zimbabwe's staple food. The price rises compounded a rapidly rising rate of inflation: the price of essential goods and foods was estimated to have increased by 25 percent in the first two weeks of January, and by 140 percent since October 1997. The rioting continued for several days as mobs of youths clashed with police, burned vehicles and looted shops. Protesters in the capital's outlying townships set up roadblocks and barricades, and pelted police and motorists with stones. The police responded by firing live ammunition and tear gas. The protesters' anger appeared to be directed against the government's management of the economy, against white Zimbabweans, and against President Robert Mugabe. Demonstrations, some violent, were reported in other parts of the country on Jan. 21, and about 300 alleged looters were said to have been arrested by police and troops in Harare's southern townships. By Jan. 26 the capital was said to have returned to a state of relative calm.

A nationwide general strike organised by the Zimbabwe Congress of Trade Unions (ZCTU) to protest against tax increases and rising food prices brought the country to a standstill on March 3-4. The ZCTU claimed that 80-90 percent of workers obeyed the strike call, resulting in the closure of shops, businesses, banks and factories throughout the country.

Reports of "running battles" between students and police at the campus of the University of Zimbabwe, in Harare (the capital), on April 24. The fighting followed an attempt by the students to march on the city centre in order to express solidarity with another student who had been shot and wounded by police during an earlier demonstration on April 22.

Riot police fired tear gas and baton-charged some 2,000 student demonstrators in Harare on May 29. The students, who had begun their protest on May 28, were demanding higher grants and an end to government corruption. The demonstrators also called upon President Robert Mugabe to resign, with warnings that they would create "another Indonesia" if their demands were not met.

Violent scenes in Harare on June 1, when students calling for an end to government corruption and an increase in state grants smashed shop windows and overturned vehicles in the city centre. The authorities on June 2 announced the closure of two higher education institutions, the University of Zimbabwe and Harare Polytechnic, for an indefinite period.

Violence erupted in Harare (the capital) on Nov. 4 when mobs of youths erected barricades, set buses alight and stoned cars to protest against the fuel increase. The ZCTU subsequently announced on Nov. 7 that it would stage strikes every Wednesday until Mugabe reversed the fuel price rise and engaged the unions in serious talks over the deepening economic crisis in the country.

Hundreds of students demonstrating against the late payment of grants clashed with riot police in the capital, Harare, on Feb 10. The students also protested against Zimbabwean involvement in the civil war in the DRC, economic mismanagement and government harassment of the free press.

The opposition National Constitutional Assembly (NCA) held peaceful marches in Harare and Bulawayo on March 13. According to the NCA the marches were held to involve the population in the drawing up of a new constitution in order to forestall government attempts to impose its own constitutional blueprint. The NCA, which was made up of opposition parties, trade unions, student bodies, human rights groups and other organisations, announced plans to hold marches in other urban areas.
Another MDC supporter, Takundwa Chapunza, died on May 18 after being attacked in the Harare township of Budiriro.

Hand grenade attack on the headquarters of the main opposition party, the Movement for Democratic Change (MDC), in the capital, Harare, on Sept. 11 caused minor damage to the building and two cars.  The MDC secretary general, Welshman Ncube, accused the police of failing to launch a serious investigation into the attack.  Ncube said that the attack was “obviously part of the terror campaign being waged by the state against any opposition to the ruling party.”

Intelligence officers and detectives accompanied by armed police raided three of the MDC’s offices, including its headquarters in Harare, on Sept. 14 and arrested four party officials.  The police said that they had raided the offices because they suspected that arms were being stored there.  A provincial magistrate issued a second warrant later in the day allowing the police to check documents and computer files.  Ncube said that no weapons had been found during the raids, and claimed that they were nothing more than harassment.

A student was killed in clashes with police on the University of Zimbabwe campus in Harare (the capital) on April 8- 9.  Police entered the campus to break up disturbances relating to student demands for increased grants.  Students stoned cars during the demonstrations and then clashed with police who responded with teargas.

Bomb attack on a ZANU-PF office in a suburb of Harare. One person died and seven others were injured. The police said that it was a “politically motivated attack” and blamed the MDC.  The MDC, however, said that the attack had been carried out by extremist ZANU-PF elements.

The Movement for Democratic Change (MDC), organised a week of nationwide strikes and demonstrations starting on June 2 in what it termed a “final push” to force President Robert Mugabe to step down. the Movement for Democratic Change (MDC), organised a week of nationwide strikes and demonstrations starting on June 2 in what it termed a “final push” to force President Robert Mugabe to step down. On the first day of the protests (June 2), opposition leaders were rounded up in police raids under the country’s security laws, which allowed the government to ban any gathering.  The police fired live bullets and teargas to disperse small-scale anti-government protests and at least three people were seriously wounded in the Highfield suburb of Harare.

The police on Sept. 12 closed down operations at the country’s only privately run daily newspaper, The Daily News, a day after the Supreme Court ruled that the newspaper group was operating illegally.  Since its launch in 1999, The Daily News had given a voice to critics of President Robert Mugabe’s rule. Speaking on Sept. 12, ANZ editor-in-chief Francis Mdlongwa described the police action as “an unprecedented attack on press freedom” and insisted that his company had always intended to comply with the law and register for a licence once the Supreme Court had issued its ruling.  The police reportedly arrested about 100 people, including a number of journalists, during a demonstration in the capital, Harare, against the closure of the newspapers.

The police arrested some 300 people in the centre of the capital, Harare, on Oct. 22 as they gathered to protest against increasing repression.  The demonstrators were members of the National Constitutional Assembly (NCA), an alliance of civic groups seeking constitutional reform.

The police broke up a series of trade union demonstrations in the country’s main urban centres on Nov. 18, arresting more than 40 people, including senior officials from the Zimbabwe Congress of Trade Unions (ZCTU).  The demonstrations against rising inflation and economic decline were timed to take place just before the presentation of the 2004 budget.

Youth militia of the ruling Zimbabwe African National Union-Patriotic Front (ZANU-PF) were reported to have gone on the rampage in the capital, Harare, on May 20, smashing windows and destroying property at the offices of the main opposition party, the Movement for Democratic Change (MDC).  The youths also blockaded the House of Assembly (the unicameral legislature) and threatened to kill a white MDC MP, Roy Bennet, who, in a fit of anger, had thrown the Justice Minister, Patrick Chinamasa, to the floor of the House earlier in the week.

The police in Harare, the capital, arrested 44 demonstrators on Sept. 1 as they tried to march in protest over a proposed law that would bar foreign human rights groups and block foreign financing for local organisations.

On Sept. 28 a further 48 women protesters were arrested on the outskirts of Harare, having walked the 400 km from Bulawayo.  The women, members of Women of Zimbabwe Arise (Woza), were arrested for staging an illegal protest.
Some ZANU-PF supporters had “invaded” the party headquarters in Harare, the capital, to protest at Mugabe’s autocratic style.
Rival ZANU-PF gangs were attacking each other in Harare and that riot police had been deployed to break up the clashes.

On May 23 the government launched an operation aimed at cracking down on black market traders and “general lawlessness” in Harare, the capital, and other major cities, including Bulawayo and Gweru.  In the first few days of "Operation Marambatsvina" (“drive out rubbish”) as many as 10,000 street traders were arrested in Harare alone.  Violent clashes were reported in many of the city’s townships as some of the traders resisted arrest. During June the government continued with “Operation Marambatsvina” with further mass bulldozing of shanty towns in the capital, Harare, and other cities, and the arrest of illegal black market traders. The Financial Times of June 7 reported that an estimated 25,000 people had been arrested and more than 200,000 made homeless.  On June 3 Miloon Kothari, the UN special rapporteur on the right to adequate housing, urged the government to halt its campaign of mass evictions, which he said was in clear violation of human rights.

A previously unknown group calling itself the Broad Alliance urged people to support a nationwide general strike on June 9-10 in protest at the evictions and arrests.  The Broad Alliance reportedly comprised the MDC, trade unions, and civil rights groups.  However, after a show of force by the authorities--which included arrests, roadblocks, and the mass deployment of heavily armed riot police--most people turned up for work as normal on June 9.  On June 10, riot police fought running battles with supporters of the strike in Chitungwiza township, south of Harare.
The leaders of the Zimbabwe Congress of Trade Unions (ZCTU) and over 100 other civic leaders and workers were arrested on Nov. 8 at the start of an anti-poverty demonstration in the capital, Harare.

A planned nationwide protest by trade unionists, called to demand economic and political reforms, was prevented from taking place on Sept. 13 as riot police stopped marches from proceeding in the capital, Harare, and other major cities and towns.Leaders of the Zimbabwe Congress of Trade Unions (ZCTU), which had organised the demonstrations, were arrested and in some cases beaten as they left their Harare office to lead a march through the city centre.  Supporters of the march were prevented from entering the city centre after the police sealed off all routes.  A spokesman for the ZCTU, Last Tarabuku, said that at least 180 people had been arrested in some of the 34 cities and towns that had planned to hold a protest.
Pakistani students handed in a note to the Indian High Commission on Feb. 4, threatening to set firs to the building if there were further riots in New Delhi.
Mobs set fire to liquor stores in both Karachi and Islamabad as a protest against the alleged drunkenness of the ruling military junta
The Islamabad newspaper New Times closed indefinitely on March 31, after members of the ruling Pakistan People's Party (PPP) had raided its offices and threatened to lynch several journalists.
Thousands of people took part in the funeral procession shouting anti-British and anti-Indian slogans, and a group of youths attempted to set fire to the British Council library.
A large number of suspended Bengali civil servants, including doctors and teachers, were rounded up in Islamabad, Karachi and Lahore on May 6 and taken to camps

A clash between students and members of the Ahmadiya sect on May 29 at Rabwah (35 miles west of Lyallpur), the headquarters of the sect, led to riots in many towns of Pakistan which continued for about three weeks, 42 people being killed.
"Pakhtoonistan movement" , a movement in favour of an independent "Sindhudesh" … 11 explosions occurred in the N.W.F.P. and also in Rawalpindi and Islamabad

Students demonstrated outside the Afghan embassy in Islamabad on Feb. 15 and tried to deliver a memorandum denouncing the Afghan Government's alleged support for terrorists and the shelter given to Mr. Ajmal Khattak.
The Syrian embassies in Rome and Islamabad (Pakistan) were attacked on Oct.11 by pro-Palestinian gunmen protesting against Syrian action against Palestinians in Lebanon

Mr Bhutto, said in a speech to about 200 of his supporters in Islamabad that martial law could not resolve the political crisis, and was cheered by a crowd of about 5,000 people at the airfield when he left for his home at Larkana.

An attack was launched on the PLO mission in Islamabad on Aug. 5, resulting in the deaths of four people.

Mr Bhutto was hanged on April 4. His death was followed by protest demonstrations and riots in all the major cities and towns of Pakistan on April 4-6.
Over 50 students from Qaid-i-Azam University (Islamabad) were arrested on June 21 after clashes between right- and left-wing students
The Pakistan authorities adopted strict security precautions in the neighbourhood of installations connected with the nuclear programme.
The Pakistan authorities adopted strict security precautions in the neighbourhood of installations connected with the nuclear programme.

About 20,000 demonstrators who marched on the Secretariat building on the following day clashed with the police, at least one man being killed and 13 injured when the police opened fire
Mr Malik Shehzada Khan, a member of the Federal Advisory Council, was shot and killed when leaving a meeting of the Council, his death being attributed to feuding among rival families
On Sept. 7, 1984, another Majlis member was killed in Islamabad in what was described as a family argument

The acting military attache at the Soviet embassy in Islamabad (the capital of Pakistan), was shot dead in his car on Sept. 16, 1986. The Soviet Union had initially alleged that the Pakistani government had been involved in Col. Gorenkov's assassination
Police shot dead five people in Islamabad (Pakistan) on Feb. 12 during demonstrations outside the American Centre in protest at the proposed publication of The Satanic Verses in the USA
50 people were reported injured, some seriously, during simultaneous clashes with armed police outside the parliament building in the capital, Islamabad.
The government on Oct. 13 ordered that all identity cards in future should indicate the holder's religion. Demonstrations in support of this move had been organized by religious parties in Islamabad
Bhutto, who was tear-gassed as she led a march from Rawalpindi to Islamabad demanding fresh elections
The government ordered an official inquiry into the cause of a fire which swept through the country's parliament building in Islamabad on Nov. 9 gutting the National Assembly
Afghan gunmen held five Pakistani schoolchildren and their teacher hostages in the embassy building in Islamabad, the rescue operation resulted in the deaths of the gunmen
India reacted strongly to what it called the "unlawful detention and brutal torturing" by Pakistani officers of an Indian diplomat based in Islamabad
A suicide bomb attack on the Egyptian embassy in Islamabad on Nov. 19 caused 17 deaths, the van containing the explosives having crashed through the metal gates into the embassy compound
Opposition parties called for nationwide strikes over "anti-people" budget. On June 24 at least three people were shot dead during clashes with police in Islamabad.

On Oct. 24 thousands of Pakistanis joined hands to form a human chain along some 600 km of the line of control as a protest against Indian rule over part of Kashmir. Similar demonstrations also took place in all major Pakistani cities
On Oct. 27-28 the opposition pro-Islamic Jamaat-i-Islami Pakistan (JIP) organized further strikes in Islamabad which quickly resulted in violent clashes between protesters and police
