At least 14 people were killed in Jakarta on Nov. 22 in religious clashes between Muslims and Catholics from the eastern Indonesian island of Ambon

Riot police fired rubber bullets and tear gas on Dec. 17 to disperse some 4,000 student demonstrators who had attempted to storm the legislature building in Jakarta. At least 116 students and 14 security personnel were reported to have been injured
A demonstration of 2,000 university students in Jakarta on March 4, hours before the visit of US Secretary of State Madeleine Albright
On March 4 a rally of 700 supporters of former President Suharto demanded an end to investigations into the Suharto family's finances
More than 100,000 Muslims marched through Jakarta on March 7 to protest against the government's failure to curb the bloodshed in Ambon.
The bombing of a shopping mall in Jakarta on April 15, for which 21 suspects were subsequently detained
Three men were injured on April 19 when a bomb exploded in the Istiqlal mosque in Jakarta, Indonesia's largest mosque.
Hostile demonstrations were held on Sept. 15 in Jakarta outside both the Australian and UK embassies

A new security law passed by the outgoing House of Representatives after seven people had been killed and at least 12 injured in clashes between troops and demonstrators against the bill on Sept. 23-24
During the debate at least 100 anti-Habibie protesters were injured in battles with the police in the streets of Jakarta, the capital
Riots erupted amongst the defeated candidate’s [Megawai] disappointed supporters on the streets of Jakarta
On Jan. 7 more than 100,000 Muslims demonstrated outside the presidential palace in Jakarta (the Indonesian capital) calling for a jihad in the Moluccas
Ayip Syafruddin, a radical Islamic leader, called on April 6 for a jihad, or holy war, in eastern Maluku province when he addressed a rally of 10,000 men in Jakarta
Daily and sometimes violent student-led demonstrations outside his house in Jakarta
A bomb in Jakarta on Aug. 1 killed at least two people and injured the Philippines ambassador and at least 20 other people whilst partly destroying the ambassador’s residence

A powerful bomb exploded on Sept. 13 in the basement car park of Jakarta’s stock exchange, killing 15 people and injuring 27. Police claimed that the bombers were linked to the Free Aceh Movement (GAM) and also worked for Tommy Suharto

Doctors rendered Suharto, 79, unfit physically or mentally to stand trial.  The decision triggered a riot as Suharto supporters, police and furious anti-Suharto protesters, many of them students, clashed outside the court.  At least one person was killed and dozens injured
At least 15 people were killed and 100 injured on Dec. 24, Christmas Eve, in a series of 15 bomb attacks on Christian churches in nine towns and cities, including five in Jakarta

On each day of the inquiry up to 10,000 protesters took to the streets outside the parliament building in Jakarta. Student demonstrations calling for Wahid’s resignation continued throughout the month
About 12,000 students on March 12 held the largest demonstration so far against President Wahid outside the presidential palace in Jakarta
On the day of the vote only about 6,000 Wahid loyalists demonstrated outside the parliament building, and without any violent confrontation with the police
Thousands of demonstrators protested on June 13-18 in Jakarta against a new labour law reducing workers’ rights
Following protests in Jakarta by Muslim groups the local military commander, Brig.-Gen. Made Yasa, was replaced
The only violence occurring during the drama of Wahid’s impeachment was the explosion on July 22 of two bombs at Christian churches in Jakarta, injuring 72 people.  No group claimed responsibility

A gunman on a motorcycle shot dead Supreme Court judge Syafiuddin Kartasasmita on July 26 in a street in Jakarta. two suspects had confessed to the murder, claiming that they had been paid and armed to commit the murder by a son of former President Suharto, Hutomo “Tommy” Mandala Putra

A suspect for a bombing in a Jakarta shopping mall on Aug. 1, in which six people were injured, was Malaysian.  He was said to be part of a jihad (holy war) group that included three other Malaysians and was also responsible for bombing Christian churches
Two bombs exploded in the garage of a shopping mall in Jakarta on Sept. 23, injuring several people but none seriously
Street demonstrations against possible retaliatory US military operations in Afghanistan after the devastating terrorist attacks on the USA on Sept. 11

Police on Oct. 8 fired tear gas and water cannon to drive off about 500 demonstrators outside the US embassy in Jakarta, who were protesting against the Oct. 7 launch of the US and UK bombing campaign against Afghanistan

On Oct. 10 police repelled about 1,000 protesters attempting to force their way into the House of Representatives. The protests continued in Jakarta on a relatively small scale (the largest being of over 5,000 people in Jakarta on Oct. 19) and were mostly peaceful

ELOCAL: offices of the UN High Commissioner for Refugees
COMMENT: Police in Jakarta on Oct. 30 evicted about 100 Iraqi and Afghan asylum seekers from the offices of the UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR).  The demonstrators had complained that their applications for asylum were being processed too slowly
A man was killed and another injured on Sept. 23 when a grenade exploded in a van outside an unoccupied US embassy residence in Jakarta.
The IDF had been responsible for few casualties but much destruction of property, its targets being bars, nightclubs and other premises in Jakarta, that were thought to be offensive to Islam

Although there was some expression of anti-US and anti-Western feeling and numerous protests were held against the war. The largest demonstration was held on March 30 in Jakarta, attended by up to 300,000 people
A home-made bomb exploded on April 27 in the restaurant area of Jakarta international airport, injuring up to 11 people, at least one seriously
A bomb exploded on Aug. 5 outside the US-owned Marriott hotel in Jakarta, the capital, killing 12 people and injuring about 150
A campaign of intimidation by Islamic extremists in Jakarta, had forced the closure of at least one Christian church.
Mobs on June 6 attacked four churches in suburbs on Jakarta, causing interior damage and injuring one priest
A bomb detonated on Sept. 9 in a van outside the Australian embassy in Jakarta, the capital, killed 10 people, all Indonesians (including a suicide bomber), and injured at least 160
Demonstrations were held in 13 cities against rises in fuel prices, in Jakarta the police fired tear gas on protesting students
There were widespread protests during February against satirical cartoons of the Prophet Mohammed which had been published on Sept. 30, 2005 in a Danish newspaper
Demonstrators of the conservative Islamic Defenders Front (FPI) on April 12 vandalised the office of Playboy Magazine publisher in Jakarta

There were also demonstrations against the bill by women’s rights and artists’ groups on the grounds that the legislation was too widely framed.  They claimed that in the guise of cracking down on hardcore pornography the bill was in reality aimed at curbing legitimate freedoms of expression

A rally of an estimated 30,000 trade unionists on May 3 in Jakarta, the capital, clashed with the police outside the House of Representatives  building.  The demonstrators, who had been protesting since May 1 against proposed changes to the labour laws
US President George W. Bush on Nov. 20 paid a brief visit to Indonesia. Nearly 20,000 troops were deployed to keep protesters against Bush’s visit from approaching the palace
Silent demonstrations by female members of ANC. Placards were carried bearing the words: "We never had it so bad."

Strike by african workers when the A.N.C. and the P.A.C. both appealed to the African population to observe March 28 as a "day of mourning" for those killed at Sharpeville and Langa by staying at home; whereas, however, the A.N.C. wished to limit the strike to one day, the P.A.C. proposed that it should be continued indefinitely until the pass system was abolished. Serious rioting in the African locations south-west of Johannesburg on March 28., when trains bringing strike-breakers home in the evening were stoned, and groups of Africans lay in ambush along the roads to stone passing cars. Police on duty at the railway stations were also stoned, an African policeman being beaten to death at Meadowlands. Crowds attacked police stations at Meadowlands and Moroko. On March 29. most Africans returned to work, including over half of those employed in Johannesburg.
Assasination attempt on Dr. Verwoerd, the prime minister, during the.opening ceremony of the Union Exposition in Johannesburg

About 20 children of all races, ranging in age from five to 18, demonstrated outside Johannesburg City Hall on May 14 demanding their parents' release, and 16 children presented a petition to Mr. Erasmus on May 16 asking that their parents should be released or tried.
Dr. Verwoerd was welcomed at Jan Smuts Airport, Johannesburg, by some 50,000 enthusiastic supporters, many of whom carried banners acclaiming the Prime Minister.

Large-scale police raids occurred in Johannesburg. Thousands of Africans were arrested during the month, mainly in Johannesburg but also in Pretoria and other centres. The detainees were described by the police as vagrants, idlers, tsotsis (thugs), agitators, and in some cases suspected criminals; by May 20 about 2,500 were reported to have been arrested in the Johannesburg area alone, mainly on charges of vagrancy and failure to carry passes or produce tax receipts.

Three-day strike called for May 29-31 met with a negligible response from the African population except in Johannesburg, where between 40 and 50 per cent of the African workers stayed at home; absenteeism was estimated at about 120,000 out of the city's total African labour force of 250,000
Five bomb explosions occurred in the Johannesburg area. Damage was done to post offices and municipal and Bantu administration cruces, one African being killed and another seriously injured.
Two electricity pylons were dynamited in a Johannesburg suburb.

A crowd of some 5,000, including nearly 2,000 university students, marched through the city centre on May 24 in protest against the Bill; some incidents occurred when Government supporters attacked demonstrators, the police using tear-gas to disperse the crowd
The supports of a steel pylon carrying electricity wires were blown up on Oct. 7-8.
A bomb exploded at the Vrededorp post office in Johannesburg on June 14.

Bomb explosion caused by a suitcase containing a time-bomb in the Johannesburg central station on July 24. Several people were seriously injured and a 77-year-old White woman subsequently died of her injuries.

Thousands of students demonstrated in protest when Mr. Brian Robertson, president of the National Union of South African Students (NUSAS) and a member of the Progressive Party, was restricted on May 11, 1966.

Counter-demonstrations by students from Afrikaans-medium universities and colleges both in Johannesburg and Cape Town on Aug. 22, the Cape Town students sit-in was terminated on that day after a majority had voted in favour of ending it.
Solidarity demonstrations by White students in Johannesburg.

Student protests included defiance of a magistrate's ban on demonstrations by about 1,000 students in Johannesburg on May 18, when 357 of them were temporarily arrested for infringing the Riotous Assemblies Act
More than 1,500 students in Johannesburg on Oct. 5 called on all Judges to resign" now that their direct authority has been undermined"

Raids by security police was  followed by the arrest of two Indians in Johannesburg on Oct. 22, when their car was alleged to have contained a large quantity of illegal political pamphlets, and at least 13 other Indians on Oct. 23, when the police confiscated 60 typewriters from two schools. One of those detained, Mr. Ahmed Timol (30), a Moslem teacher, had committed suicide by jumping to his death from the tenth floor of the main police building in Johannesburg.

Serious student unrest occurred at both non-White and White English-language universities in April-June 1972, leading to forceful police action against demonstrators in Cape Town, Johannesburg and elsewhere, following an anti-Government speech made by a Black student leader at one of the Bantu universities. On June 6 Mr. P. C. Pelser, the Minister of Justice, banned all political meetings and marches in 16 cities until July 8.
Violent clashes between English-speaking and pro-Government Afrikaans-speaking students took place in Johannesburg
Strike by textile workers in Johannesburg for the lack of recognition of black workers and their trade unions.

An Israeli official was killed and 37 people were injured, most of them passers-by, when a security guard at the Israeli consulate in Johannesburg, who was later found to have a history of mental instability, held the consul-general and 20 of his staff hostage for 16 hours on April 28-29.
Protesting students in Soweto burnt out a police car and damaged three other cars.

Clashes between demonstrators and police began in Soweto on June 16 when police tried to stop a procession of a large number of Black pupils carrying placards reading "Away with Afrikaans". Police shot 8 children and Black youths killed a Black policeman and a White official. Protest march by some 300 Witwatersrand University students was on June 17 broken up by police. On June 18 the riots spread to several other localities; Alexandra Township (where 12 Africans were reported killed). The unrest at Soweto died down by June 20.
2,000 Black students was on a strike in Soweto in protest against the language rule.
Fires occurred at a number of schools for Africans, including two at Soweto.

Demonstrations by over 20,000 Africans, both from Soweto and from Alexandra (north of Johannesburg), attempted to prevent Black workers from going to work and to stage a march to the "White" city of Johannesburg in order to demand the release of those arrested during the June riots. Shops were burned and cars overturned in Soweto. An appeal to the SRC not to repeat the march to Johannesburg on Aug. 5 was defied by more than 5,000 youngsters, who were dispersed by police with firearms and tear gas.
Three-day strike was observed in Soweto from Aug.23 to 25 by between 150,000 and 200,000 workers.

Black groups, mainly Zulu migrant workers armed with clubs and knives, clashed with demonstrators objecting to their going to work in Johannesburg, while other Africans attacked the police whom they suspected of inciting the migrant workers. At least 21 Africans were reported to have died in such clashes, while 10 others were killed by the police.
Six more people were killed in Soweto on Aug.30
Strike by Black workers in Soweto.

Demonstrations against the visit to South Africa by Dr Henry Kissinger, the US Secretary of State, staged by schoolchildren. Police were reported to have shot dead six African schoolchildren and to have wounded 23 others by opening fire at four schools after the children staged the demonstrations.

Anti-apartheid demonstrations by some 1,500 young Blacks in central Johannesburg on Sept.23 police arrested about 400 people after petrol bombs and stones had been thrown and several shooting and stabbing incidents had occurred. In Soweto police on the same day opened fire in a clash with some 500 students.

Five-day strike called for Nov. 1 by the SSRC met with only limited response, they succeeded in enforcing the indefinite closure of shebeens (illegal bars) whose owners they accused of "perpetuating the subjugation of Blacks".
Explosion in a restaurant in the city on Dec. 7, 1976. Isaac Seko (21) was sentenced in Johannesburg to 12 years in prison for causing the explosion.
Militant students made another attempt to dissuade other students from attending classes and writing examinations, and police dispersed altogether some 6,000 young demonstrators with tear gas.
Some 10,000 students protesting against rent increases in Soweto were dispersed by police with tear gas
A bomb exploded in a shopping complex in central Johannesburg, on Nov.27 (injuring at least 17 persons).

A Black state witness at a trial of 18 alleged ANC members was the target of a grenade attack made on his house at Alexandra Township (near Johannesburg) on Jan.20 after the security police had removed him "for his own safety".
Attack on police stations in Soweto by ANC.
2 Blacks were killed in an explosion near a White residence early on Aug.12, 1980.

The Johannesburg-Soweto railway line was disrupted on Oct.15, 1980, by the explosion of two bombs (with eight other bombs being found and made harmless at the same time) after a call for a mass strike in protest at rent increases and also at the award to Dr Koornhof of the freedom of Soweto
A suspected terrorist blew himself up with a hand grenade during a police raid on Nov.21, 1980.
Sabotage of SASOL fuel installations and the attack on Booysens police station (Johannesburg) in 1980.

Crowds of Coloured students who were demonstrating in Johannesburg on June 3, 1981, to show their solidarity with Mr Aziz Jardine, president of the students' representative council at the Christian Botha school, who had been arrested the previous week. violence erupted again the following day. Coloured secondary school pupils boycotted classes on June 5 in protest at what they called "police brutality", and further school boycotts received considerable support.

Police used tear gas against hundreds of Blacks who were leaving a service at the Catholic Regina Mundi Cathedral. The police alleged that youths had begun to stone passing vehicles, but this was denied by Black leaders who claimed that the police action was unprovoked.

Police raid in Soweto to investigate allegations that some burials were "being abused for ANC propaganda purposes where Mrs Sisulu, wife of Mr Walter Sisulu (a former ANC leader), was briefly arrested.
ANC bomb attack on pylon on the Johannesburg-Soweto railway.
Attack on police stations in Soweto by ANC.
Police barred 47 local and overseas journalists from entering Soweto, and tear gas was later fired to disperse crowds at the Regina Mundi Cathedral.

60 buses, 25 police vehicles and six private cars in Soweto had been damaged by stone-throwing, which was apparently an expression of anger against blacks who had ignored the call of the Azanian People's Organization (Azapo) to stay away from work on June 16

Mr Pares Molefe Malatji (24), who had been arrested for an undisclosed reason on July 4, 1983, and held at the Protea police station in Johannesburg under the Criminal Procedures and Evidence Act, was shot dead on July 5.

Disturbances at several schools in Soweto in late July, followed the transfer of a popular white head teacher in one school and the replacement of three black teachers by three whites at another. Five teachers were beaten up by students, and 31 black students and four community leaders were arrested on Aug. 1
A bomb exploded on Aug. 6 at a synagogue in central Johannesburg four hours before Mr Marais Viljoen, the State President, was due to attend a ceremony there
Explosion at the Johannesburg office of the Department of Foreign Affairs
A man was wounded in Johannesburg on June 15, reportedly by the accidental detonation of an explosive device which he was planting.

The Aug. 28 elections were similarly marked by violence, with disturbances reported by police at 19 polling booths, mainly in the Transvaal. Police baton-charged several hundred protesters at Lenasia, an Indian suburb in western Johannesburg. It was reported that supporters of the National People's Party had participated with police in attacks on anti-election protesters.

ELOCAL: Offices of Department of Internal Affairs
COMMENT: Bomb explosion on Sept. 3 at the central Johannesburg offices of the Department of Internal Affairs. Several people injured.
Petrol bombs were thrown at the house of the newly appointed mayor of Soweto, Mr Edward Kunene
A bomb exploded at SADF offices in central Johannesburg on May 28, in which 16 people were injured.

Demonstration of some 400-500 students protesting at a magistrates' court, where over 100 people were due to appear on charges of illegally demonstrating outside the mayor's house. The mayor's house was set alight on the morning of July 17. In another district of Soweto, a bus carrying West German and US tourists was stoned, and was escorted from the township by police.

Several students were arrested and 10 were injured in a clash with police on Aug. 12, at the (mainly white) University of the Witwatersrand in Johannesburg. Police baton-charged students who were protesting about the state of emergency, while students attacked the police with stones.
800 schoolchildren in Soweto were arrested and held overnight in a police operation to end a schools boycott.

Violence broke out in the streets of central Johannesburg as a crowd of about 3,000 blacks protested against the execution after Mr Malesela Benjamin Moloise, a black South African upholsterer, poet and member of the banned African National Congress (ANC), who in 1983 had been convicted of murdering a black policeman, was hanged on Oct. 18, 1985. Several people was injured.
Four-day strike for better pay and conditions by auxiliary workers and nurses at the the central hospital for the black township of Soweto and other black townships in the Johannesburg area.

Four people, including a student nurse at the hospital who had reapplied for her job during the strike, died after their house in Soweto had been set alight by unidentified men on Nov. 24, apparently in response to the nurse's action.

Violence in the black township of Alexandra on Feb. 15-18, 1986, with an official figure of 22 deaths and unofficial reports of nearer 80 deaths. The unrest began with protest demonstrations during the funerals of a local leader of the Black Consciousness movement, the Azanian People's Organization (Azapo), who had reportedly been murdered, and of a youth who had been shot dead by a nightwatchman. The houses of black policemen and councillors were attacked, and there were reports of clashes between police armed with shotguns and youths throwing petrol bombs.

A hand grenade was thrown at police who arrived to disperse a crowd protesting at evictions being carried out by the Soweto Council on Aug. 26 at White City, one of the poorer areas of the township. road blocks had been set up in anticipation of protests, and that a police patrol had fired without provocation at a crowd of demonstrators. according to official figures, 20 people were shot and killed by the security forces, and one black councillor was killed by residents of the township. According to unofficial figures released by the UDF, the Soweto Civic Association and COSATU, the death toll was nearer 30 with some 200 people injured.

Eleven people were killed in violent clashes in the course of a strike by black transport workers against their employers, South African Transport Service (SATS), in the Soweto area of Johannesburg. On April 22 seven people died in clashes involving strikers, police and residents of Soweto who had staged a 'stay-away' in support of the SARHWU workers.On the same day the employers effectively crushed the strike by summarily dismissing about 16,000 striking workers, and proceeded to engage new staff.

A hand grenade was thrown into a parade ground at a police training school in Soweto, killing 1 police recruit and injuring 64 other recruits. The banned Pan-Africanist Congress (PAC) claimed responsibility in this instance.

Police searched the federation's headquarters, Cosatu House, in Johannesburg, twice during the transport workers' strike, making over 400 arrests, following allegations that SARHWU workers who did not support the strike were abducted and tortured there. The second raid, on April 29, followed the discovery of the bodies of four murdered transport workers who had broken the strike.
Powerful bomb explosion at the headquarters of COSATU rendered the building structurally unsound.
Powerful car-bomb explosions near a magistrates' court in Johannesburg on May 20, 1987, which killed four police officers. ANC claimed responsibility for the bomb explosions.

Afrikaner Resistance Movement (AWB) and a multiracial group of students staged rival demonstrations at Johannesburg airport prior to the IDASA delegations arrival. the IDASA delegation were prevented by a government directive from holding a press conference on their return to South Africa from Dakar on July 21.

A car bomb exploded near the Witwatersrand Command headquarters of the South African Defence Force (SADF) in central Johannesburg on July 30, 1987, injuring 68 people and causing extensive damage. The ANC was widely believed to be responsible. The attack was the latest in a series of bomb explosions in the city

Mr Sicelo Godfrey Dhlomo, a worker for the Detainees' Parents' Support Committee, was murdered in January 1988, soon after he had appeared on a secretly-made CBS television documentary in which he claimed to have been tortured by police while in detention; a few days before his death he was interrogated at police headquarters in Johannesburg, after which the police issued a statement saying that he had made a sworn affidavit accusing the CBS of instructing him to fabricate his testimony to the camera.
Soweto residents protesting at evictions carried out by the council clashed with police

Three day strike protest against the proposed legislation and at the February 1988 restrictions on trade union and other political activity. An estimated 90 per cent of black workers participated in the protest in the Durban area, 50 per cent in Pretoria and in Johannesburg. The three-day demonstration was thus the country's largest strike protest. The government claimed that 10 deaths had occurred in relation to the stayaway over the three days.

Strike organized by NUMSA, The National Union of Metalworkers, in early August 1988, in support of a pay claim. Wage negotiations had broken down after the employers' organization, the Steel and Engineering Industries Federation of South Africa had rejected union demands for a 23 per cent wage increase and made a counter-offer of 14-17 per cent.

A body, belatedly identified by Stompie's mother after it was brought forward in mid-February, was said by police to have been stabbed through the neck and dumped on waste ground on Jan. 6. Stompie" Moeketsie Seipei, had confessed to being a police informer, responsible for the deaths of four comrades, while being questioned at Winnie Mandelas house.  Police searched the Mandela home on Feb. 19, making the first arrests there. Two members of the "team", the "coach" Jerry Richardson (aged and Jabu Sithole, were the first to be charged, on Feb. 21, with abduction and murder.

The others whose deaths were associated with the affair (see $#33A)  were a Soweto doctor, Abubaker Asvat, shot in his clinic on Jan. 27. Any alleged link between Winnie Mandela and the killing of Asvat was denied by a man accused of his murder, who pleaded guilty in a Johannesburg court on Feb. 22 but gave his motive as petty burglary.

The others whose deaths were associated with the affair (see $#33A) were Maxwell Madondo, apparently a Mandela "team" member, whose body was found in Soweto on Feb. 13. A rival group to the group of Winnie Mandela was suspected of killing Madondo.

ELOCAL: House of a member of a rival group
COMMENT: 14-year-old girl, Finki Mosomi, who died in what was described as a reprisal raid on Feb. 22 on the house of a member of a rival group suspected of killing Madondo (see $#33C).

David Webster, a (white) university lecturer and prominent human rights activist, who had recently contributed to a report highlighting the increase in attacks on anti-apartheid campaigners, was shot and killed outside his Johannesburg home on May 1. Widespread coverage of the killing linked it with a new tactic of right-wing terror.
Peaceful mass march on Sept. 16 in Johannesburg to deliver a memorandum on police violence to the police security branch headquarters.
Peaceful demonstration on Sept. 21 by 1,000-2,000 students at Witwatersrand University as part of the End Conscription Campaign (END) in protest at the detention of four of the campaign's organisers.

A large-scale rally attracted some 60,000-80,000 people to a Soweto soccer stadium on Oct. 29, in an unprecedented display of public support for the still banned ANC, whose flag was displayed prominently, as was that of the South African Communist Party. Walter Sisulu, in the keynote address, demanded one-person-one-vote elections to a constituent assembly.

A Right-wing group calling itself "White Wolves" on July 4 claimed responsibility for a bomb attack during the rush-hour on July 6 injured 27 people at a Johannesburg bus station much used by commuters from the townships.

Tens of thousands of township residents stayed away from work on Aug. 27 as a mark of protest over the township violence, coinciding with a mass funeral to bury seven of the dead. Of an estimated 515 deaths in the townships, 126 were killed in Soweto.

Police raided COSATU headquarters in Johannesburg and arrested the trade union federation's general secretary, Jay Naidoo, and two other union leaders after a police agent had been apprehended and paraded during a news conference given by the union. The officials were charged with kidnap and assault and were ordered to return to court in October.

Indiscriminate massacre of commuters took place on a Johannesburg-Soweto train on Sept. 13 at least 26 people were shot or hacked to death, and well over 100 injured. A former member of the South African Defence Force (SADF), Sgt. Felix Ndimene, said that he had served in a special forces unit which had carried out the massacre in September 1990 of 26 passengers in a Johannesburg-Soweto train, and were instructed to kill the commuters as part of the war against the ANC.
Sixteen people were killed by unidentified gunmen in apparent reprisal attacks in Soweto and nearby townships on Oct. 28 after an Inkatha supporter was killed by a mob.
Protest rally in Johannesburg by Inkatha supporters in response to a rally to relaunch the ANC Youth League.

The ANC demanded "total and verifiable" evidence that hit squad activities had been stopped after a prominent black lawyer was killed on Feb. 15 by a booby-trapped tape-recorder posted to his Soweto home. The victim, Bheki Mlangeni, had been engaged in investigations into the activities of police "death squads" and the much-criticized Civil Co-operation Bureau (CCB) of the South African Defence Force, which, it had been alleged, was formed to eliminate anti-government activists. The bomb was apparently addressed originally to Dirk Coetzee, a South African former police captain then living in Lusaka, Zambia.

Battle for political control of the townships, included the reported killing of at least 60 people in Alexandra township. In one incident in Alexandra, gunmen appearing to be Inkatha supporters slaughtered 15 mourners at a funeral, including seven members of the pro-ANC Congress of South African Students (COSAS). Earlier, pro-ANC mobs were said to have hunted Zulu-speaking workers from an Alexandra hostel whom they accused of starting violence on March 10 in which 28 people died.
Some 500 armed Inkatha supporters attacked a workers' hostel near the Mandelas' home in Soweto. Four men were killed and some 40 wounded in clashes there on April 14.

TARGET1: Supporters of Zulu Inkatha Freedom Party
COMMENT: Up to 60 people were reported killed on the weekend of April 27-28 when ANC supporters clashed with supporters of Chief Gatsha Buthelezi's predominantly Zulu Inkatha Freedom Party in Soweto and Alexandra, on the outskirts of Johannesburg. The continuing fighting between the two groups was seen as having wrecked a five-point plan to end the violence, agreed at a meeting between Buthelezi and Mandela on March 30.

Mass rally in Johannesburg city hall where Mandela told the mass that the government had failed to meet its obligations, and promised "mass action" to secure the release of remaining political detainees.
Two nephews of Mandela killed by gunmen at their home in Soweto on May 3.
A wave of unexplained and apparently random bomb explosions occurred in central Johannesburg on May 17, wounding 12 people, black and white. Police found and defused a number of limpet mines.

King Goodwill Zwelithini launched a strong attack on Mandela and the ANC at a mass rally of some 40,000 Inkatha followers armed with traditional weapons, in a Soweto sports stadium. The king demanded an apology from the ANC whose followers, he said, had insulted the Zulus.

Between Aug. 9 and 13, 24 people were killed in the Alexandra township of Johannesburg after Inkatha supporters arrived, armed with spears and clubs, to take part in what was billed as a non-political clean-up operation to remove rubbish from the township.

An incident on Sept. 8 in Thokoza, Johannesburg, in which Inkatha supporters were ambushed by unidentified attackers, left 23 people dead and sparked retaliatory attacks throughout Johannesburg's townships. The ANC denied responsibility for the attack, and there was renewed suspicion about a "third force" deliberately inciting enmity among the black population.

A two-day nationwide strike on Nov. 5-6 against the imposition of value added tax (VAT--introduced in September) on basic foodstuffs, health care and essential services was supported by at least 80 per cent of the workforce in manufacturing and industry. The figure, which represented over 3,700,000 workers, was confirmed by employers.
A series of attacks on white teachers by a group of pro-PAC black youths after the education than 30 white teachers from schools in Kathlehong, a township south of Johannesburg, on Feb. 1.

Campaign of mass action with a two-day nationwide strike, the government made various attempts during August to convince the ANC of its good faith. There were reports that 90 per cent of the workforce stayed away in the Johannesburg region.

Assassination on April 10 of Chris Hani, general secretary of the South African Communist Party (SACP) and a member of the national executive committee (NEC) of the African National Congress (ANC). Hani was shot as he stepped from his car at his home in Boksburg, a predominantly white suburb of Johannesburg. Janus Walus, a Polish-born emigré who settled in South Africa in 1981, was arrested within an hour of the assassination. Walus had been a member of  neo-nazi Afrikaner Resistance Movement (AWB) since 1986, and it was confirmed that the assassination had been part of a white right-wing conspiracy.

Violent incidents were reported at three of the mourning  demonstrations and up to 17 people were reported to have been killed, including three who died when police opened fire on a crowd outside a police station in Johannesburg.

ACTOR1: Heavily armed supporters of the AVF
COMMENT: The Johannesburg World Trade Centre, was stormed by a group of heavily armed supporters of the Afrikaner Volksfront led by Eugene Terreblanche, leader of the AWB brandishing shotguns and revolvers seized the main negotiating chamber where they attacked black delegates and journalists, their actions unhindered by the 1,000 armed police officers on duty.

TARGET1: Supporters of the Inkatha Freedom Party
COMMENT: Between July 2 and July 13, more than 220 people were killed in the East Rand in fighting between supporters of the ANC and the Inkatha Freedom Party, with Tokoza and Kathlehong, townships east of Johannesburg, the scenes of the worst incidents.
Inkatha anti-election demonstration in Johannesburg on March 28 degenerated into violence in which at least 31 people died and 276 were injured.

Bomb attack raising fears of a concerted white right-wing campaign to disrupt the elections. The first serious attack was on April 23 when a massive car bomb exploded near the ANC offices in central Johannesburg, killing nine people and injuring 92.

A car bomb exploded at Jan Smuts airport in Johannesburg. Of the 16 casualties, four were seriously injured. Some hours after this last attack, police arrested 31 members of the neo-nazi Afrikaner Resistance Movement (AWB) including the alleged brains behind the bombing campaign.

Marches organized on June 6 in Johannesburg, Durban, East London, Pietermaritzburg and other towns were attended by more than 100,000 workers. Mandela made an unexpected appearance at the Johannesburg rally. On June 19 there was a half-day general strike observed by tens of thousands of people in Johannesburg, Cape Town, Durban and 24 other major towns. This was intended to put pressure on employers in negotiations on new labour relations legislation, organized through the National Economic Development and Labour Council (Nedlac). The action was also to oppose any government moves towards privatization of state assets.

Demonstration by thousands of supporters of the Inkatha Freedom Party had been called to mark the anniversary of the 1994 "Shell House killings", when eight Inkatha supporters were killed by security guards from the ANC's Shell House headquarters.

South Western Joint Civics Association (Sawejoca) called a “stayaway” in protest against rent and service charges. Violence broke out after hundreds of youths built barricades in the streets to prevent residents going to work, and intensified as police tried to clear the barricades. Witnesses said that some protesters were heavily armed and at least one police officer was shot in the back by an automatic rifle.

Up to six people were killed and several injured in random shootings linked to a march through Johannesburg of up to 18,000 supporters of the predominantly Zulu Inkatha Freedom Party. The march was to commemorate the third anniversary of the so-called Shell House massacre. The marchers, many in traditional Zulu dress, defied a ban on the carrying of traditional weapons and chanted anti-ANC slogans. Police said that the deaths had occurred in a Soweto hostel and at various of the rally’s starting points.

TARGET1: ANC member of the National Assembly
COMMENT: Duma Nkosa, an ANC member of the National Assembly (the legislature) and chairman of the parliamentary minerals and energy portfolio committee, was shot and critically injured by four gunmen in Johannesburg on Sept. 29.
Strikes against privatisation by municipal workers belonging to the South Africa Municipal Workers’ Union (SAMWU).
Six people were killed in violence on polling day, five of them at a polling station in Katlehong, east of Johannesburg.

In July the opposition Pan-Africanist Congress (PAC) launched a controversial campaign to house thousands of homeless people on wasteland in Bredall, near Kempton Park, east of Johannesburg. Hundreds of police officers gathered at the site on July 5 to begin removing people from the site.  About 200 people were arrested, including a number of PAC organisers. A private security company, backed by some 300 armed police officers, began clearing shacks on the land on July 12.  A last-minute intervention by church groups headed off clashes between the last of the squatters (some 400 people) and security forces.
Series of bomb blasts in Soweto on Oct. 30 by extreme right-wing racist groups, which killed one person, damaged a mosque and an Islamic school, and destroyed railway connections with Johannesburg.
The centre of Johannesburg was brought to a standstill on Nov. 30 as tens of thousands of demonstrators took to the streets in an unprecedented protest against violent crime.
