ACTOR1: Movement for the Restoration of Democracy
COMMENT: The opposition rejected these concessions, insisting on the legalization of political parties as a precondition to negotiation. A crowd estimated at between 100,000 and 200,000 gathered near the royal palace, and police opened fire; the resulting death toll was estimated at between 35 and 150
The King had approved the draft constitution in September. However, on Oct. 21 he had submitted a number of amendments to the document, provoking demonstrations in Kathmandu

ELOCAL: South Asian Association for Regional Co-operation office
COMMENT: On Oct. 7 five ethnic Nepalese members of the Tsogdu who had fled in May formally sought political asylum and threatened a hunger strike in front of the Kathmandu offices of SAARC
A month of political unrest followed the killing of at least 12 people during a one-day general strike in the capital, Kathmandu, on April 6
Katmandu was paralysed on Aug. 14 by a general strike, one in a series of demonstrations in August organized by left-wing opposition partie
On Aug. 30, supporters of Adhikari's United Communist Party of Nepal (UCPN) staged demonstrations in Katmandu in protest against the Supreme Court's decision
On Sept. 13, left-wing opposition parties organized a general strike in protest… violent protests by left-wing demonstrators in Katmandu on Sept. 22
On June 15 over 10,000 NCP supporters demonstrated on the streets of Kathmandu in protest over alleged electoral irregularities
Some 15,000 retired Gurkhas (Nepalese nationals who served in the British army) protested in Kathmandu on July 16 over the size of their pensions
On July 3 that 3,000 former British Gurkha soldiers and their families had marched in Kathmandu

A general strike was called for Oct. 7 by the underground Communist Party of Nepal (Maoist) in protest against the death on Sept. 24 of politburo member Suresh Wagle, allegedly in police custody. ...At least 11 people were injured by bombs

Violent street protests on Dec. 26-27 in Kathmandu, the capital, in which five people were killed, 120 injured and many Indian shops attacked.  Students organised a general strike starting Dec. 28 which paralysed the capital.
Two bombs had been set off in Kathmandu on April 2 causing no injuries. A general strike in Kathmandu on April 6, called by the Maoists
At least 100 opposition supporters were temporarily detained on April 17-18 during anti-government demonstrations in Kathmandu

Massacre of royal family. Including the king and queen, eight members of the royal family died on the spot, whilst Dipendra and Dhirendra Shah, 51, King Birendra’s youngest brother, both died in hospital on June 4
When Gyanendra was crowned on June 4 after Dipendra’s death, bewildered and angry crowds rioted in the streets of Kathmandu and two people were shot dead by the police
Tens of thousands of people marched on Sept. 14 in Kathmandu, in rallies supporting the peace talks and calling for an end to the fighting
Two bomb attacks struck the outskirts of Kathmandu in early December.  There were no injuries in the bombing of a Coca Cola factory, but two people died after a bomb attack on a carpet showroom
Gunmen on Dec. 15 shot dead a Nepalese security guard from the US embassy in Kathmandu ...believed to be related to the Maoist uprising
Maoists were held responsible for a bomb explosion on March 29 in Kathmandu in which 27 people were injured.
There were two small bomb explosions in Kathmandu on the night of April 22
A general strike called by the Maoists for April 23-27 brought Kathmandu and much of Nepal to a standstill, largely because of a campaign of intimidation by the rebels
A strike called by the Maoists on Oct. 28 caused severe disruption in Kathmandu.
October included at least four bomb blasts in Kathmandu, the capital, which injured about 30 people.  An explosion on Oct. 12, the day the new government was sworn in, killed one person
16 journalists detained in Kathmandu under the provisions of anti-terrorism legislation
Thousands lined the streets of Kathmandu on Jan. 9 to see the customary first public appearance of six-month-old Prince Hridayendra
On Jan. 26 when three gunmen shot dead the inspector general of the Armed Police Force (APF) Krishna Mohan Shrestha, his wife, and a bodyguard on the street in Kathmandu
Several hundred students took part in an anti-government demonstration on April 9 in Kathmandu
Student unions called strikes for April 20, 23, 28-29 but called off the last day’s action

The opposition political parties, supported by students, had mounted almost continuous demonstrations nationwide and in Kathmandu, the capital, against both the Chand government and the King.  It was reported that about a hundred people were injured on May 11
The UCPN, the NCP, and three other parties on June 20 mounted a mass rally in Kathmandu
On Aug. 28 Maoist gunmen shot dead a army Col
An estimated 60,000 people on Aug. 29 attended a peace rally in Kathmandu, the capital, urging the two sides to return to negotiations.
About 1,000 people were arrested in Kathmandu on Sept. 4-6 as the five major opposition parties, held protests demanding that King Gyanendra reinstate the legislature
A general strike called by the CPN—M on Sept. 18-20 effectively paralysed life in Kathmandu
Five political parties—all represented in the dissolved legislature--organised a demonstration in Kathmandu, the capital, on Dec. 16 to protest against “regression”.
During January there were almost daily student-led demonstrations in Kathmandu, the capital, against King Gyanendra and in favour of a republic.
The five parties of the Joint People’s Movement (JPM) agitating against the government appointed by King Gyanendra and for a return to democracy held demonstrations throughout the month in Kathmandu

The CPN—M called a general strike on May 18-20 that paralysed Kathmandu and disrupted communications throughout the country, seriously affecting the economy.  At least 45 people were killed in fighting during the strike, including 31 rebels
The Maoist-aligned All Nepal National Independent Student Union—Revolutionary, held a strike that closed schools across the country for 12 days

The rebel underground Communist Party of Nepal—Maoist (CPN—M) imposed a blockade on Kathmandu.  ...the blockade of Kathmandu, previously largely unscathed in the civil war, was widely regarded as a successful show of strength by the CPN—M
On Aug. 20 a Maoist shot and critically injured a policeman in Kathmandu before detonating two small bombs outside a tax office
The police on Sept. 1 shot dead two men in Kathmandu, the capital, as they tried to control rioters angry at the murder of 12 Nepalese hostages by insurgents in Iraq
Maoists were thought to be responsible for two bombs thrown on Sept. 10 at the American Information Centre in Kathmandu, causing minor damage but no casualties

King Gyanendra on Feb. 1 dismissed the government led by Prime Minister Sher Bahadur Deuba and assumed direct control of Nepal, declaring a state of emergency and putting troops on the streets of Kathmandu
From March 8 there were several attempts, both in Kathmandu, the capital, and other towns, to hold demonstrations protesting against Gyanendra’s seizure of power

At least 4,000 people demonstrated on May 22 in Kathmandu, the capital, against the King’s direct rule, the first protests organised by the seven-party alliance.  Peaceful marches demanding the restoration of democracy were also held in other major towns

Some 30,000 people took part in demonstrations on Sept. 20 in Kathmandu, the capital, calling for an immediate end to the fighting between government forces and Maoists. Women demonstrators told that the police had assaulted them, biting, punching, and kicking so that about a dozen were seriously injured. ...concern over beatings and other excessive force used by the police against demonstrators

The police arrested about 80 political activists on Jan. 19 and shut down both landline and mobile telephone communications, on the eve of a demonstration organised by the parties in Kathmandu. ...the thousands of police and troops effectively preventing a mass demonstration
The Maoists called a seven-day general strike from Feb. 5, but lifted it late on Feb. 8, saying that it had served its purpose in disrupting the polling
In a statement at a rally on Feb. 19 in Kathmandu, King Gyanendra called on the political parties to restart “the stalled democratic process”

Massive daily pro-democracy demonstrations--in which 18 protesters were killed and hundreds injured—which brought Kathmandu (the capital) and other towns to the brink of open revolt. ...crackdown on the demonstrations in Kathmandu in which 21 people died
Tens of thousands of people on April 27 attended a jubilant victory rally in Kathmandu which was addressed by the opposition seven-party alliance (SPA)

The resolution proclaimed Nepal, previously the world’s only Hindu kingdom, a secular state.  It was reported that Hindu groups mounted a number of demonstrations in Kathmandu by the end of the month protesting against this change
Some 200,000 Maoist supporters were bussed into Kathmandu on June 2 for their first mass rally in the capital, which passed peacefully
The police on Sept. 20 arrested about 50 activists of the Nepal Maoist Victims Association and injured several in fighting at a demonstration in Kathmandu

Overthrow of Military Regime by a popular uprising in Khartoum, which started with a protest meeting at Khartoum University against the government on October 22., followed by demonstrations the next days. The demonstrations increased in violence on Oct. 24 assuming an anti-American aspect; demonstrators attacked the U.S. Embassy and the U.S. Information Service library. General strike from October 25., which ended after the formation of the new Government on October 30.
Thousands of people demonstrated in Khartoum, demanding a purge of the armed forces and the trial and execution of the members of the military junta.
Serious rioting occurred in Khartoum on Dec. 6-7, 1964, when fighting broke out between Southerners and Northerners.
Demonstrations occurred in Khartoum on Feb. 6 in response to an appeal by the Information Minister to the people to defend the Government

Protest demonstrations by trade union members took place in the capital on Feb. 19-20, a general strike on the latter date largely bringing the country to a standstill, which ended up in mass protest demonstrations in Khartoum by workers and left-wing students on Feb. 23-24.

Repeated clashes in Khartoum between Communists and the Moslem Brotherhood after  The Umma Party and the N.U.P. demanded the dissolution of the Communist Party on Nov. 12, after a student had publicly declared that he was proud of being a Communist and an atheist and had made derogatory remarks about the Prophet Mohammed. On Nov. 17 a student burned himself to death as a protest against the proposal.
Protest demonstrations in Amman and Khartoum against executions of three members of the illegal Moslem Brotherhood
During the Assembly's session police using tear-gas prevented demonstrators from entering the precincts of the House
Protest march against the Assembly's decision, by the Sudan Bar Association, which has strong left-wing affiliations. Several of the marchers was injured in clashes with the police.
Hostile demonstrations against the Emperor Haile Selassie of Ethiopia during a state visit to Khartoum

Opposition demonstrations and pro-government demonstrations led to the arrest of several hundred persons, and troops were guarding Government buildings and bridges across the Nile and preventing Opposition members from entering the Assembly building,

Opposition demonstrations and pro-government demonstrations led to the arrest of several hundred persons, and troops were guarding Government buildings and bridges across the Nile and preventing Opposition members from entering the Assembly building,
50,000 people demonstrated against the President of Chad

TARGET1: Government of Mr. Mohammed Ahmed Mahgoub
COMMENT: Bloodless coup
Several thousand persons demonstrated in favour of the new regime in Khartoum on June 2.
Several explosions took place in Khartoum, including one at the American Library on July 19
Explosion at a hotel in Khartoum. Radio Khartoum announced on Aug. 17 that a "reactionary plot" had been foiled and several civilians and military men arrested

Military coup attempt when Major el Atta supported by the Presidential Guard, who surprised General al Nemery with other members of the Revolutionary Command Council at his residence and placed them all under arrest. Major-General al Nemery's return to power was announced late on July 22 by Omdurman Radio, which said that forces loyal to him, led by Lieutenant Mohammed All Kerbassi and supported by other officers who had refused to join Major el Atta's Revolutionary Council, had seized Khartoum radio station, occupied the presidential palace, freed General al Nemery and arrested all leaders of the July 19 coup. A total of 38 persons had been killed and 119 wounded during the counter-coup.
Demonstration called for July 22 by the Communist Party

Big counter-demonstrations calling for the return of General al Nemery; and the latter had thereupon been reinstated with the help of soldiers of the same units which had first supported Major el Attn. Major el Atta

Eight armed Palestinian Arab terrorists of the Black September organization broke into the Saudi Arabian Embassy in Khartoum on March 1 during a diplomatic reception, held the Saudi Arabian Ambassador, the Belgian and Jordanian Charges d'Affaires, and the American Ambassador and counsellor as hostages, and murdered the U.S. and Belgian diplomats before surrendering after 60 hours to the Sudanese authorities.

University students demanded "the return of the Army to barracks in order to leave politics to the politicians". After clashes between demonstrating students and police and Army units had caused the death of a soldier and a civilian, the University of Khartoum and other institutions of higher education were closed on Aug. 30-31.The unrest, however, continued during the next few days. On Sept. 3 Army units and special police took action against student demonstrators, of whom several hundred were arrested.
Strikes were called by six trade unions, including those of the railwaymen and the food industry, in protest against the armed forces' "use of violence against the young people".

Rebellion led by Lieut.-Colonel Hassan Hussein Osman, which occupied a local radio station. The station was regained after the Minister of Agriculture and deputy secretary-general of the SSU, led a tank force against the occupied radio station and regained it from rebel control after a battle lasting an hour and 40 minutes, during which a number of rebels were killed or wounded. A second rebel group, waiting to attack the presidential palace, was routed by loyal troops, and scattered skirmishes took place in parts of Khartoum throughout the day.

Coup attempt by armed civilians, which was supressed by loyal troops to President Nemery. Grenades exploded in many parts of the city, as armed civilians fired shots indiscriminately and advanced on the airport: but the President and his party succeeded in escaping to a village at some distance from the capital, from where he organized resistance to the rebels. The Sudanese Government stated that the Sudan had been the target of" an act of banditry designed by Libya to create chaos and to effect the overthrow of the legally-constituted Government" of the Sudan. The complaint also stated that Libya had provided training, arms, ammunition and transport for about 1,000 mercenaries, some of whom had confessed to being trained in Libya while others had said that they had been trained by Libyan instructors elsewhere.

Student riots which took place in Khartoum on Aug. 7-8 were officially stated to have caused "much damage and destruction" and to have been instigated by "vanquished remnants" of political organizations who continued "to plot and conspire against the revolution".
Five-day strike by railway workers demanding wage increases and a reduction in the cost of living, was an increase in the price of staple foods and fuel.
Assasination attempt on President Nemery and eight cabinet members in May 1980.

Grenade attack on the Chad embassy in Khartoum in which one embassy employee was killed and three people injured. The person who perpetrated the crime made a complete confession which showed that Libya was behind the attack.
Demonstration from students at Khartoum university against price raises which were broken up by police using batons and tear gas.

Students in Khartoum staged a protest in response to the announcement that sugar subsidies had been abolished immediately on the recommendation of the World Bank. On Jan. 6 a group of 5,000 students began a march which the police unsuccessfully attempted to break up with tear gas; three students were seriously wounded, one of whom subsequently died. Two days later the country's four universities were closed.
Student protests concerning social conditions in the capital, which had suffered a series of power cuts throughout August. Classes resumed on Sept. 24

Strike by doctors began on March 1, 1984, in protest over low pay and the deteriorating situation in the health service. The doctors union was subsequently reinstated, after 70 of its members had been detained briefly, and doctors resumed work on April 14.
University of Khartoum was closed in April 1984 for an indefinite period following a lecturers' pay dispute and student demonstrations against the President.
Violent clashes between members of the Brotherhood and opposing factions

Rioting broke out in the major cities as a reaction to price increases. Shops and banks were attacked on March 28 and the US embassy was stoned, while police and troops used tear-gas and fired shots over the heads of demonstrators; a police spokesman stated that some 2,000 people had been arrested. The offices of the SSU were reportedly sacked on March 29, and tear-gas was used on demonstrators attempting to march on the US embassy.

Khartoum doctors staged a strike on March 30-31 in protest at the methods used by the authorities to suppress the demonstrations, although emergency services were maintained. The doctors claimed that at least eight people had been killed, and that police had used electrified truncheons as well as guns to control the riots.
Pro-Nemery rally, organized by the SSU on April 2, attracted between 2,000 and 3,000 people
Anti-government demonstration, led by doctors' and lawyers' professional associations, attracted an estimated 20,000.

General strike specifically intended to bring down the government. The armed forces had assumed control in a coup on the evening of April 6, when Gen. Swar el Dahab declared that the action had been taken 'to stem the flow of blood' and to 'stand on the side of the people'. The general strike ended on April 8.

Demonstration in Khartoum of bank employees protesting at alleged corruption in the Central Bank, following which the Governor of the Central Bank, Mr Mahdi el-Fakhi, resigned on July 22 and was replaced by Mr Ismail Misbah el-Mekki.
About 20,000 people demonstrated at the Egyptian embassy in Khartoum in protest at the Egyptian government's refusal to expel former President Nemery to face trial in Sudan.

Outbreak of gunfire in Army barracks in north Khartoum and nearby Omdurman on Sept. 25-26, 1985, was later described by the transitional government as the start of a coup attempt, which was foiled by paratroopers.

Demonstrations in the capital in protest at the closure of schools, the lack of transport, and power cuts. Oil imports were frozen in mid-February, and on March 12 petrol prices were increased by an average of 60 per cent. The price of bread increased by 33 per cent on March 23; this was followed on March 27 by a similar, unannounced increase in sugar prices.

In mid-July thousands of school pupils demonstrated in the streets of Khartoum against the lack of school teachers and shortages of equipment, after which schools in the capital were closed, finally reopening on Oct. 27.

Student-led demonstrators protesting at food prices and shortages of consumer items during six days of unrest in Khartoum (the capital) in late October and early November 1986, in which one person was killed, and over 300 arrests were made. On Oct. 29 two men, described as 'leading custodians of the defunct regime' (a reference to the regime of President Nemery), were arrested reportedly for having instigated the riots.
Bloodless military coup carried out on June 30, 1989, by a group of Army officers
Protests at Khartoum university against a background of continuing civil war and the growing threat of famine.
Government troops on Dec. 22 killed 13 inhabitants of a shanty-town in the southern outskirts of Khartoum who were resisting its demolition.

Street protests in early October giving rise to some of the worst violence since Bashir seized power in 1989. The violence began in Khartoum and Omdurman and then spread to the Adbara, Wad Medani and Gedaref. Reports said that demonstrations were broken up by police firing tear gas and by pro-government militants belonging to the NIF. Dozens of communists and trade union members were also reported to have been arrested.

Three days of rioting in Khartoum on Sept. 12-14. The disturbances were the most serious anti-government riots since Lt.-Gen. Omar al-Bashir's military regime seized power in mid-1989. Violence erupted when thousands of students, demonstrating against the arrest of three of their fellow students, clashed with police. Police were reported to have used tanks, tear gas and live ammunition to disperse the students. The government claimed that the riots had been organized by the banned Communist Party. It also blamed "foreign quarters". Commentators understood this to be a reference to Eritrea and Ethiopia, relations with these two countries having deteriorated in recent months.

Riots in and around the capital on Sept. 1 in which two people were killed. The riots were prompted by the refusal of bakeries to produce bread in protest over new government regulations relating to size and price of loaves, as a result of which there had been no bread in the city since Aug. 30. The situation was inflamed by the fact that in the previous fortnight Khartoum  had also suffered water shortages and power cuts.
Anti-government demonstrations in Khartoum on Sept. 3 which followed the demonstrations on september 1. Protesters set fire to vehicles and attacked government buildings.

A succession of bomb explosions in Khartoum, the capital, in early July caused extensive damage but no casualties according to official statements. Up to 300 arrests were reported in the following days. On July 6 state television reported that 16 bombs had been planted, of which seven had exploded.

US Navy ships in the Red Sea launched missile attacks against the Shifa Pharmaceutical Industries plant in north-east Khartoum (the Sudanese capital). The US claimed that the plant was financed by bin Laden and was used to make precursor chemicals for VX nerve gas.

Up to 100,000 people took to the streets of Khartoum, the Sudanese capital, on Aug. 4 in an officially orchestrated demonstration to protest against the deployment of Western forces in the Darfur region.
Serious rioting and inter-ethnic violence broke out in Khartoum after the confirmation of Mr Garang's death. Garang died in a helicopter crash on July 30.

ELOCAL: Home of Mohammed Taha Mohammed Ahmed
COMMENT: Mohammed Taha Mohammed Ahmed, the editor-in-chief of Al Wifaq, an independent newspaper, was abducted from his home in Khartoum, the capital, on Sept. 5.  His decapitated body was discovered in the city on the following day.  Taha had been severely criticised in Sudan in 2005 for publishing articles denounced as blasphemous.

Several Africans were killed in Leopoldville on May 13 in fighting between Luluas and Balubas in the African suburbs of the city; troops were moved into the quarter, and a curfew and ban on public meetings imposed. Other less grave incidents meanwhile continued to be reported from various parts of the country.
Disorders involving members of the Puns party, who were protesting against M. Lumumba's failure to ensure the election of M. Bolikango as Head of State

ACTOR2: Supporters of M. Kalonji's wing of MDC
COMMENT: Demonstrations by M. Bolikango's supporters on June 28 were dispersed by the police. There were separate demonstrations in the city on June 28, also necessitating police intervention, by adherents of M. Kalonji, in support of his demand for the admission of his wing of the M.N.C. into the Government
Fighting between Bakongo and Bayaka tribesmen broke out in Leopoldville on July 1

On July 4 the employees of the Office des Transports du Congo (Otraco) went on strike in the capital and other towns along the river because they had not been paid an "independence bonus." The strike of dock workers, on the other hand, continued on July 5-6, and a number of the senior European staff of Otraco were manhandled by strikers.
Unrest in Leopoldville police, and for a time a number of Belgian police commissioners were locked up by their own men.

The situation in Leopoldville worsened abruptly in the morning of July 7, the city being virtually taken over by mutineers, now heavily armed. According to a subsequent statement by M. Bomboko, this development had begun with a rumour that Russians were landing at the airport with Belgian assistance; a group of soldiers who had gone to the airport to look for them returned to the city and started holding up cars and searching Europeans for arms. By 9.30 a.m. soldiers had closed the port, preventing refugees from crossing to Brazzaville, while others occupied the telephone exchange and blocked the road to the airport. Soldiers on foot and in vehicles. Belgian troops from the airport entered Leopoldville without resistance on July 13.

Widespread strikes broke out following the announcement of a Government decision to raise the minimum legal wage by 30 per cent; because this only affected limited classes of workers, many others immediately demanded similar wage increases.

ACTOR1: Members of the youth movement of President Kasavubu's Abako party
COMMENT: Demonstrations in the city by supporters of a federal system in the Congo, led by members of the youth movement of President Kasavubu's Abako party, following growing signs of opposition to M. Lumumba.

A number of people were injured when police opened fire in Leopoldville during rioting which started when police attempted to raid the Abako headquarters in the African quarter of Dendale; M. Lumumba himself was reported to have been slightly injured when he attempted to intervene.

M. Makoso, editor of the Courrier d'Afrique, was arrested again by Congolese police in Leopoldville, his newspaper being suspended; the newspaper Congo, a strong supporter of M. Lumumba's policies, was left the only daily paper appearing in the capital.

Congolese police and gendarmerie in Leopoldville opened fire on crowds demonstrating against M. Lumumba during the opening ceremony of the conference of African States, which had been convened by the Prime Minister. The demonstrators, comprising members of Abako, Puna, and the Kalonji wing of the M.N.C., as well as strikers and unemployed men, carried banners calling for federation. After M. Lumumba had entered the hall where the conference was being held, the police began driving back the crowd, beating some demonstrators with their rifles; stones were thrown, and some of the police opened fire on the fleeing crowd.
Troops opened fire on a demonstration led by the Abako youth movement, killing at least three children and one man.

M. Lumumba, accompanied by some 20 armed soldiers, made an unsuccessful attempt to enter the Leopoldville radio station, being refused admittance by the Ghanaian U.N. guard under a British officer, Lieutenant George Short.

ACTOR1: Members of President Kasavubu's Abako party
COMMENT: Demonstration against M. Lumumba was held by members of President Kasavubu's Abako party, who marched through the streets to the Central Post Office, where they were dispersed by troops with fixed bayonets.

Bands of Congolese soldiers, some apparently drunk, moved into the African quarter of Leopoldville and throughout the day terrorized the inhabitants, many of whom were beaten and others arrested; U.N. patrols did not intervene and M. Kamitatu's provincial police remained in their stations. The outbreak was reported to have followed an overnight clash between soldiers and Bayaka tribesmen in a village outside Leopoldville.

M. Kamitatu, with his wife and children, was arrested on the orders of Colonel Mobutu, who stated that he had taken this action because secret military and civil documents had been handed over to the U.N. authorities by a Minister in M. Kamitatu's provincial government.

Demonstrations against the arrest of M. Kamitatu. Moroccans on guard duty outside the U.N. headquarters fired over the heads of a crowd of 300 Congolese who had gathered to demand U.N. intervention to secure M. Kamitatu's release. Later, when the crowd moved towards Colonel Mobutu's house, Ghanaians took up position between them and the Congolese soldiers guarding the house and escorted the crowd back to the African quarter, where Congolese soldiers began to disperse them. Four people were killed during the day's incidents.
Attempt by Congolese soldiers to force an entry into the Ghanaian Embassy to expel Mr. Welbeck, with fatal casualties on both sides.
Demonstrations by about 1,000 youths in Leopoldville on Jan. 15 led to the sacking of the British Embassy in Leopoldville.

Following another demonstration demanding the liberation of M. Antoine Gizenga, seven Opposition deputies were arrested on Sept. 25, while on the following day the Minister of the Interior (M. Joseph Maboti) banned all political activities of the M.N.C. and the Parti solidaire africain (the party of M. Gizenga's supporters) in Leopoldville.

Arrest of the leaders of the Congo's three largest trade unions--M. Andre Bo-Boliko (Union of Congolese Workers), M. Alphonse Kithima (Confederation of Free Trade Unions of the Congo), and M. Remy Siwa (General Federation of Congo Labour)--on charges of endangering the security of the State. All three union leaders had been leading a renewed strike of civil servants and teachers in Leopoldville, where a state of emergency had been proclaimed on October 21. According to press reports, some of the teachers had not been paid for a year.

M. Anany (the Defence Minister) announced on June 8 that more than 100 persons had been arrested in connexion with bomb attacks in Leopoldville, that the explosives had come largely from Algeria, and that the perpetrators of the attacks were connected with the NLC in Brazzaville. Five "terrorist" members of the NLC were condemned to death on July 4 by a military tribunal.
The Burundi Embassy in Leopoldville was sacked on Aug. 10 by a crowd of young Tshombe supporters who also demonstrated against Chinese aid for the rebel movement.

The Portuguese Embassy in Kinshasa was sacked by about 400 demonstrators, many of them refugees from Angola, on Sept. 24, and the Portuguese Charge d'Affaires, Dr. Antonio Rosano Garcia, was seized and beaten up by the demonstrators, but later set free on orders from President Mobutu.
2,000 persons demonstrated in Kinshasa in favour of a break in diplomatic relations with Portugal.
Violent demonstrations took place in Kinshasa the same day (Aug. 14) and led to the pillaging and burning of the Belgian Embassy and the overthrowing of a statue of King Albert I.
Letter bomb which had been posted in Paris were delivered on Sept. 17 to the British Embassy in Kinshasa, a security officer being injured in Kinshasa.
More than 200,000 people demonstrated against the U.S. Government in front of the U.S. Cultural Centre in Kinshasa on June 18 against alleged involvement of US government in planned coup attempt.
Rioting at the Institute for Applied Technology in Kinshasa.
Month-long strike by students.
Three days of demonstrations by students over conditions and inadequate levels of government support.

Two explosions in Kinshasa on March 28 by opposition movements. The government said the bombings had been part of 'a Libyan-backed destabilization campaign' which was said to be operating through Libya's diplomatic representation in Brazzaville (Congo).
Rally by UDPS was broken up by security forces. According to the UDPS three people were killed and some 200 arrests were made.

In early April students had rioted in Kinshasa, the capital, showing frustration over lack of political reform, corruption in politics, and low levels of grants; they set fire to 25 buses and called for Mobutu's resignation.Schools and colleges were closed on April 7 in response to the unrest, but on April 12 Mobutu announced large increases in student grants and civil service wages.
Peaceful student demonstration calling for a national conference had been dispersed by troops using tear gas.
Demonstrations outside home of Tshisekedi wa Mulumba, leader of the Union for Democracy and Social Progress, after he was announced as the new prime minister by Mobutu..
Police fired on protestors on Sept. 2. According to opposition figures "dozens" of people were killed.

Rioting began on Sept. 23 when soldiers occupied Kinshasa airport in protest at low pay. They were joined by civilians angry about hyperinflation and the delays in the national conference. On Sept. 24 France and Belgium (the former colonial power) sent troops to Kinshasa after rioting left 30 people dead. Although the presence of the foreign troops helped to restore calm, official sources said that 117 people had died by Sept. 25 and at least 1,500 had been injured.
Troops fired tear gas and shots to disperse a crowd of 15,000 demonstrators in Kinshasa after the dismissal of Tshisekedi.

Bomb explosion in Kinshasa on November 11, which killed 17 people, was blamed by the opposition on supporters of Mobutu. The explosion destroyed a bar in an area known for its strong support for the Sacred Union.
Demonstrations in Kinshasa after Prime Minister Jean Nguza Karl-I-Bond on Jan. 19 ordered the suspension of the national conference until further notice.

On the night of Jan. 22-23, the national radio station in Kinshasa, Voix du ZaVOL38re, was occupied by a group of soldiers who broadcast statements calling for the dismissal of Nguza's government and the resumption of the national conference. The station was recaptured after about three hours by soldiers loyal to the government, after firing in which two soldiers were killed.

In the following days, groups of soldiers were reported to have gone on the ramn Kinshasa, looting shops and businesses. On Jan. 29 Nguza said that the government would restore order and deal with the army's concerns over food and pay.

A strike call by the Sacred Union on Feb. 3 was taken up by workers across the country. By mid-February food stocks were said to be running low; according to local reports, soldiers were sent to Matadi, the country's main port, to unload ships and transport goods to the capital. On Feb. 24 Monsengwo Pasinya, president of the national conference, indicated that Nguza had abandoned the round table idea but was still expected to demand a reduction in the number of delegates to the conference

Peaceful pro-democracy demonstration led by radical Roman Catholic priests. According to the ZaVOL38re Human Rights League 30 people were killed and over 100 injured when troops opened fire on thousands of peaceful protesters. The government put the death toll at 17.
Troops surrounded ministries in Kinshasa,ordering civil servants to go home. A sharp rise in tension was reported in the city.
Demonstrators ignored advice from opposition parties and went ahead with marches in Kinshasa on Dec. 18 which reportedly led to five deaths in confrontations with security forces.
On Jan. 16 Kinshasa came to a halt as an anti-Mobutu general strike took effect. During a further stay-away on Jan. 18, 11 anti-Mobutu demonstrators were killed when troops opened fire.

Troops at a camp in Kinshasa had gone on the ramhrough the capital, complaining that the new banknotes with which they had been paid (issued by the Mobutu-controlled Treasury but declared invalid by the Prime Minister) were worthless, since traders refused to accept them. Mobutu's decision to send in the presidential guard, to quell rioting by disaffected troops, led to serious fighting in Kinshasa and hundreds of deaths. French troops were deployed to evacuate expatriates after the French ambassador, Philippe Bernard, was killed by machine-gun fire. The Governor of Kinshasa on Feb. 1 banned all public meetings in Kinshasa, the capital, in response to the continuing violence. Diplomatic sources put the figure at between 80 and 300, but sources close to the HCR estimated that at least 1,000 people had been killed when Mobutu's presidential guard was deployed to quell the riot.

On Feb. 24 troops surrounded the People's Palace, the venue for HCR meetings, and held its 1,000 occupants hostage. The entrance of the building was blocked with tanks, a trench was dug around it and supplies of food and water cut off. According to Lt.-Gen. Eluki Mongoa Aundu, Chief of Staff of the Armed Forces, the action was to draw attention to the "disgraceful situation created by the Z 5,000,000 note". He demanded that the note be approved by the HCR. The siege ended on Feb. 26 when the soldiers withdrew, declaring that the HCR "understood" their demands.
On June 12 Tshisekedi had been detained for 10 hours, on unspecified charges, and his supporters had staged violent protests in the capital, Kinshasa, the following day.

All-out one-day strike on Jan. 19, to turn Kinshasa into a "ghost town" called by Tshisekedi in protest at Mobutu's action which had announced the dissolution of the HCR and the National Assembly on January 14.

Gustave Malumba Mbangula, Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of the Interior, was reportedly attacked by an armed assailant in Kinshasa on Sept. 19. The minister was not injured, although a youth in the vicinity was shot. None of the attackers was apprehended.

People demonstrating in favour of Tshisekedi’s earlier announcement that he intended to dissolve the legislature was dispersed by soldiers used tear-gas and armoured cars to disperse the demonstrators. A number of areas of the city were reported to be in a state of anarchy after the soldiers actions.

On May 14 the city was gripped by a general strike as most of the population heeded a call by opposition leader Etienne Tshisekedi for a “dead city” day in protest at Mobutu’s refusal to leave office.

On the night of May 16-17 members of the presidential guard, apparently including Mobutu’s youngest son Capt. Mobutu Kongolo, went on the rampage, targeting those whom they regarded as traitors. According to local Red Cross workers 177 people were killed in relatively isolated revenge attacks and looting incidents.

On May 17, as ADFL troops entered Kinshasa, Kabila announced the establishment of the Democratic Republic of Congo in which he would be president. ADFL troops entering Kinshasa were welcomed as liberators by thousands of the city’s inhabitants. The rebels had taken Camp Tshashi, the final stronghold of the FAZ, by the morning of May 18, and made rapid progress in mopping up the last isolated pockets of resistance.

On May 26 the new government, citing a need to ensure security, ordered the suspension “until further notice” of the activities of all political parties within Kinshasa. The restrictions included a ban on all public demonstrations. The move followed demonstrations in protest at Tshisekedi’s exclusion from the government.

Etienne Tshisekedi, the leader of the Union for Democracy and Social Progress (UDPS) and an active opponent of the Mobutu regime, was detained on June 26. His arrest came within hours of an address to a rally of several thousand students in Kinshasa, an act which contravened the ban on political activity. The detention provoked angry protests by Tshisekedi's supporters who had expected the UDPS to play a significant political role in the post-Mobutu era. Stone-throwing crowds were dispersed by troops firing into the air, and the tension was reduced when Tshisekedi was released on June 27.
More demonstrations took place on May 28 and 29, but the support for Tshisekedi appeared to be dwindling.
On Sept. 29, shells fired in Brazzaville struck Kinshasa, capital of the neighbouring Congo Democratic Republic (formerly ZaVOL43re) across the River Congo, killing 17 people.

Gen. Masasu Nindanga, President Laurent Kabila's Special Adviser for security and the acting Army Chief of Staff, was arrested by troops in Kinshasa on Nov. 25 and taken to an unknown destination. Kabila was reported to have accused Nindanga of maintaining a private militia and of "fraternising with enemies of the state".
Clashes involving rival army factions on Nov. 28. Up to 20 people was reprted killed. Much of the violence occurred in the area of Kinshasa around the President's office and the central bank.
Sporadic gunfire and isolated battles took place in the suburbs of the capital between Aug. 26 and Aug. 29 as rebels were forced withdraw towards Kinshasa by Angolan troops.

Joseph Olenghankoy Mukundji, the national president of the opposition movement, the Innovational Forces of the Sacred Union (Fonus), was arrested in Kinshasa on March 30.  According to the DRC newspaper Le Potentiel, Mukundji was detained because of his call for a “ghost city day” protest on April 8.

A fire in an ammunition dump at the Ndjili International Airport in Kinshasa led to a series of explosions which killed at least 101 people and wounded 216 others on April 14. On April 19 a previously unknown group called the Public Salvation Army (PSA) claimed responsibility for the blasts.  In a letter posted from Belgium the PSA said that it would target the presidential palace next. However, the DRC embassy in Belgium said that it did not believe the group’s claim, reiterating that the explosion was an accident.

President Laurent-Désiré Kabila was assassinated in confused circumstances in Kinshasa, the capital, on Jan. 16.  According to the authorities, Kabila was shot by one of his bodyguards, Rashidi Kasereka, who was then immediately shot dead by other bodyguards. Belgian sources suggested that his assassination was related to a planned purge of senior military officers after serious military reverses in the south-eastern province of Katanga. They claimed that senior officers had ordered Kabila’s death in order to avoid being dismissed from their posts. President Robert Mugabe of Zimbabwe declared that Kabila had stood for the DRC’s sovereignty and hinted at foreign involvement in the killing. On May 23 the chief state prosecutor, Luhonge Kabinda Ngoy, released his report into the assassination of Laurent-Désiré Kabila.  Ngoy said that the murder “was part of a planned coup attempt” involving Uganda, Rwanda, and the Rwandan-backed Congolese Rally for Democracy (RCD) group.  Uganda, Rwanda, and the RCD. Ngoy said that since the start of the war, “hostile groups were formed, in the country and abroad, to make a coup d’etat”, adding that “aides in the entourage of the late President were recruited by these groups”.  He said that “hostile groups” behind the murder also killed 11 Lebanese nationals resident in the DRC capital Kinshasa “to hide evidence”.

A man in a military uniform opened fire in the presidential palace in the capital, Kinshasa, on Nov. 4, setting off a firefight in which two presidential guards and the gunman died.  Three other security officers were injured in the attack, which occurred when President Kabila was not in the palace.

Serious rioting erupted in the capital, Kinshasa, on June 3-4 as mobs attacked compounds housing soldiers from the UN Organisation Mission in the DRC (MONUC), blaming the peacekeepers for failing to stop the capture of Bukavu by the renegade soldiers.  At least 12 people died in the rioting, some killed by MONUC troops.

Heavy gunfire and tank shelling was reported in Kinshasa on June 11 as government troops put down a coup attempt by disgruntled members of the presidential guard. In the early morning of June 11 an officer identified as Maj. Eric Lenge appeared on state radio and declared that his forces had “neutralised” President Kabila’s transitional government.  He urged the army not to resist and said that his “people are all over the country”.  Lenge and around 30 soldiers were routed from the state broadcast station and retreated to a military base in Kinshasa before fleeing to the Bas Congo region.  Security forces arrested 12 of the fleeing men.

Violent clashes between protesters and riot police after announcement of possible election delay in the sprawling neighbourhood of Masina left between four and nine people dead, including a policeman who was burned alive by a mob.

Violent protests at the delay to the elections erupted in the capital, Kinshasa, the central town of Tshikapa, and the eastern town of Goma on June 30.  The Guardian of July 1 reported that up to 12 people had died during clashes between protesters and riot police.

Tensions had been high in the run-up to the elections, with clashes and rioting reported in Kinshasa (the capital) and in the east.  The Financial Times of July 31 reported that the elections had highlighted a split emerging between the east and the west of the DRC.  In the east, the area most affected by decades of war, Kabila was by far the most popular candidate.  However, Bemba’s popularity had risen during the campaign, particularly in the west.

Clashes erupted in the capital, Kinshasa, on Aug. 20 as it was announced that the historic presidential election in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) would go to a second round on Oct. 29 after incumbent President Joseph Kabila had failed to secure an outright majority. fighting had erupted in Kinshasa between members of Kabila and Bemba’s security forces and that at least five people had died.  On Aug. 21 the army had ordered all troops in the city to lay down their arms after the UN Organisation Mission in the DRC (MONUC) sent in armoured vehicles to remove foreign diplomats trapped by fighting around Bemba’s home.  Fresh fighting between the two sides broke out in central Kinshasa on Aug. 22, resulting in as many as 17 deaths. fighters loyal to Kabila and Bemba had started joint patrols with Eufor R. D. Congo units in Kinshasa on Aug. 23 in order to enforce a MONUC-imposed ceasefire.
Two people were killed in clashes with the police on Oct. 29. But voting was relatively peaceful.
Four people had died in fighting in Kinshasa between the opposing political forces, armed supporters of Kabila and Bemba, on Nov. 11.

On Nov. 18 a petition challenging the results was filed on behalf of Bemba at the Supreme Court of Justice in Kinshasa.  The hearing opened on Nov. 21 but was disrupted when heavy fighting erupted outside the court.  Following the unrest, Kabila met William Swing, the UN Secretary-General’s special representative for the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), and warned that unless the UN could persuade Bemba to withdraw his troops from Kinshasa, he would order the army to disarm the Vice President’s men by force.  Swing met Bemba on Nov. 22 and the following day 600 soldiers loyal to Bemba who were stationed in central Kinshasa were deployed to a camp outside the city.

Protest demonstrations against trial took place outside the court in which one person was killed and 50 arrested.

Large-scale strike called by a Joint Action Committee (J.A.C.) of the Nigerian trade union movement broke out on June I in protest against the Federal Government's delay in publishing its decision on the report of the Morgan Commission--a body appointed in October 1963 to review the wage-structure of daily-paid and low-income workers.
Dr. Okpara's request for postponement of the elections was supported by a demonstration of about 2,000 UPGA followers in Lagos on Dec. 28.
Protest strike by UGPA against the election. Clashes occurred between police and demonstrators. Many polling booths having been destroyed by demonstrators.
At least seven people were killed in rioting in the city's northern suburbs and many injured.
Military coup by rebel officers.
The Principal of Lagos City College was shot, though not killed. Ibos people were also leaving Lagos, after there had been several attacks on prominent Easterners.
The Nigerian Army seized a consignment of arms and ammunition from a ship in Lagos harbour destined for Port Harcourt and Onitsha in Eastern Nigeria.
In two bomb explosions in Lagos on July 2 four persons were killed and fourteen injured

At least five persons were killed and 64 injured by a bomb exploding in a street at Yaba, five miles from Lagos city centre. In Biafra it was claimed that this explosion had been carried out by "Yoruba freedom fighters.
Friendship aircraft shot down over Lagos on Oct. 7, 1967 during the war with Biafra.

Students protested against the introduction of a Youth Labour Service by the Nigerian government  on Feb. 26 in Lagos, where damage was caused to university property and five demonstrators were arrested.
Military coup. Bloodless.

Unsuccessful coup attempt by a small group of army officers. General Murtala Ramat Mohammed, who had become Nigeria's head of state in a bloodless coup on July 29, 1975 , was assasinated together with his aide de camp, orderly and driver, while driving to his office at Dodan Barracks in Lagos on the morning of Feb.13., and that other casualties included a Colonel Dumoje "shot and wounded in another location in Lagos on his way to the office.

ELOCAL: British high commission and the US embassy
COMMENT: The British high commission in Lagos was attacked by students on Feb. 17 after news of Lieut.-Colonel Dimka’s visit there on the morning of Feb.13 had become known in Lagos and rumours had spread that he had taken refuge in a foreign diplomatic mission. (At the same time an attack was made on the office of Reuters news agency, situated in the same building; the agency's chief correspondent in Nigeria. The US embassy in Lagos was also attacked on Feb.17 by demonstrators accusing the Central Intelligence Agency of involvement in the coup attempt.

Peaceful demonstrations called by NUNS in support of its position after increase in university fees which rose from a policy of general cutbacks in public spending necessitated by a sharp drop in oil exports. demonstrators who had left the Lagos University campus were driven back by riot police using tear gas. In the ensuing disturbances one student died after being shot in the leg by a police officer, and those injured were reported to include 36 other university students and 10 staff members, two secondary school pupils and nine policemen..

Serious rioting was reported to have occurred in several areas of Lagos on a number of occasions in the two weeks following the April 18 disturbances, in which non-student elements were said to have taken advantage of the prevailing tense atmosphere to indulge in what the police described as "lawlessness, hooliganism, looting, robbery and arson". According to The Guardian of May 12, some of those involved in the riots were protesting against rising food prices.
A coup led by high-ranking army officers on Dec. 31, 1983, overthrew the civilian government of President Shehu Shagari

Maj.-Gen. Muhammad Buhari was deposed as Head of State in a 'palace coup' carried out by senior Army officers early in the morning of Aug. 27, 1985. One policeman was reported killed as a result of the coup, which was otherwise without casualties.

Mr Dele Giwa, a prominent journalist and editor of the outspoken weekly world affairs journal Newswatch, was killed by a parcel bomb delivered to his home in Lagos on Oct. 21 by men claiming to be from the Newswatch office. The motives for his assassination, and the identity of the perpetrators, were unclear.

Three people were killed, several injured and 85 arrested in street battles between police and citizens in Lagos on Nov. 18-20, 1987. These were triggered by a traffic warden attempting to arrest a motorist and exacerbated by the killing of two brothers by a police constable. Calm was restored when the two policemen were arrested and a four-man judicial panel, headed by Mr Justice Oladipo Williams, was set up by the then Lagos state Governor, Navy Capt. Mike Akhigbe, to investigate the riots.

Some demonstrations and critical press coverage against the British Government's policy towards South Africa when The UK Prime Minister, Mrs Margaret Thatcher, visited Lagos and Kano on Jan. 7-8, 1988.

A Libyan diplomat was murdered in Lagos on Jan. 24, 1988. The police reported that he had been killed by armed robbers, but Libyan officials expressed concern about their security and raised the possibility of Chadian involvement.

More than 100 people were reported killed during widespread rioting in late May and early June, which had been sparked off by student protests against economic austerity measures. at least five people were killed when police opened fire on a crowd attacking their station in Lagos..

The first series of riots began in Lagos on May 4 when commuters protested against a 500 per cent fare increase imposed by taxi and minibus drivers who were trying to cope with an acute fuel shortage and with spiralling inflation. The demonstrators set fire to vehicles and blocked access roads with barricades made from burning tyres. In the next 10 days they were joined by hundreds of unemployed youths who looted shops, damaged cars and demanded money from passers-by.

Peaceful demonstration of students calling for the resignation of Ibrahim Babangida's military regime degenerated into violence when police fired on the demonstrators with rubber bullets and live ammunition, killing at least seven people.
Fresh rioting was reported in Lagos on May 21 after the arrest on May 18 of Beko Ransome Kuti, chairman of the Campaign for Democracy (CD), and of leading CD activists Femi Falana and Baba Omojola.

Nationwide strike was begun on Feb. 3 by state government workers demanding a 45 per cent pay rise, on a par with that awarded to federal government staff in June 1992. By Feb. 18 the administrations in Lagos State, Yobe and Katsina had acceded to this demand; union leaders insisted that action would continue elsewhere.

Lagos was brought to a halt as thousands heeded a strike call at the beginning of a week of civil disobedience. Protesters erected burning barricades, sealing off the central business district, and called for the immediate installation of Moshood Kashimawo Olawale "MKO" Abiola of the Social Democratic Party (SDP), who, according to unofficial but widely accepted figures, had been the winner of the annulled presidential poll. On July 6 the military government, the National Defence and security Council (NDSC), gave the army and the Lagos city authorities 24 hours to restore order after riot police killed at least 11 demonstrators. The following day tanks were despatched to quell the angry crowds and calm was reportedly restored.

Strike action in support of Abiola, called at the end of August by the main unions, was suspended in early September to give the government time to assess the unions' demands and the effect of their action. The Nigerian Labour Congress (NLC) called a temporary halt to its general strike on Sept. 2 and the National Union of Petroleum and Natural Gas Workers (Nupeng) ended its action on Sept. 6. The combined effect of the industrial action had been to cripple the economy, bringing major towns to a halt, paralysing the transport system and causing a serious fuel shortage.

The Campaign for Democracy (CD) called for renewed protests beginning on Sept. 29 and ending on Oct. 1, which it set as the target date for the resignation of the National Interim Government and the installation of Abiola as President. the streets of Lagos were virtually empty, most businesses and banks were closed, and government offices were staffed by only a handful of employees, despite warnings from the police that they would deal "ruthlessly" with those participating in "illegal rallies, processions or demonstrations". Other international news agencies reported the violent break-up of a demonstration on Sept. 29 when the police used tear gas to disperse protesters.

Military takeover late on Nov. 17, following a four-hour meeting with Abacha and other senior military figures, Shonekan announced his resignation as head of state and commander-in-chief of the armed forces. Abacha immediately took over both positions.

On June 12 the newly established National Democratic Coalition (Nadeco) called for a week of peaceful protest. This sparked scattered demonstrations in Lagos and parts of Abiola's home region in the south-west.
An estimated 1,000 market women, accompanied by hundreds of trade unionists, were tear-gassed by police as they marched through Lagos's business district to demand Abiola's release.
Further clashes between police and demonstrators on June 28, seven people were injured and hundreds arrested.

On July 4, members of the National Union of Petroleum and Natural Gas Workers (Nupeng) began a strike in protest against the annulment of the June 1993 elections and the detention of Abiola. Although declared illegal by the government. On July 12 the strike was joined by the Petroleum and Natural Gas Senior Staff Association of Nigeria (Pengassan). the police on Aug. 18 sealed off the headquarters of the three organizations and on Aug. 19 arrested 25 leading opposition activists. Nevertheless the strike continued, encouraged on Aug. 23 by a court decision which reinstated the union leaders until an appeal was heard. Decision by union leaders on Sept. 4 to suspend their pro-democracy strike, which had brought the oil industry to a standstill for two months.
The strikers and pro-democracy campaigners clashed with police on 18 when up to 20 people were killed in a confrontation with riot police in Lagos.
Serious rioting and deaths were reported in Lagos on July 27

Aug. 3 was marked by the beginning of an indefinite general strike in support of Abiola called by the Nigerian Labour Congress (NLC). In Lagos, where government offices and business were closed, three people were killed in clashes with the police. On Aug. 4, NLC leaders decided to suspend the strike to allow for negotiations with the government.

A bomb went off at Lagos airport on Nov. 17, the first anniversary of Abacha's seizure of power, causing damage but no casualties. In a nationwide televised address that day Abacha defended his regime's record and policies, promising to reform government before returning the country to democracy.

During the weekend of Dec. 16-17, armed arsonists were reported to have set fire to part of the Guardian newspaper building in Lagos. The office of The News magazine was reported at the end of December to have been set alight, causing publication to be suspended.

Kudirat Abiola, the senior wife of Chief Moshood "MKO" Abiola, the presumed winner of the 1993 presidential elections, was killed on June 4 when unidentified assailants opened fire on her car in a northern district of Lagos near her home. Her driver was wounded.

Two bombs had exploded in the city during December, apparently aimed at Col. Muhammad Marwa, the military administrator of Lagos and close aide of Gen. Sani Abacha, the country’s military ruler. No one claimed responsibility for the attacks. NADECO had been blamed for a series of anonymous bomb attacks on military personnel between December 1996 and May 1997 (See $#35B).

Two soldiers died and 29 others were wounded on Jan. 7 by a bomb which detonated as their army bus passed a bus stop in Lagos. Two civilians were also injured in the attack. NADECO had been blamed for a series of anonymous bomb attacks on military personnel between December 1996 and May 1997 (See $#35B).

There was a further bomb attack on Feb. 12 when five soldiers and three civilians were injured by a nail-bomb in a Lagos street. NADECO had been blamed for a series of anonymous bomb attacks on military personnel between December 1996 and May 1997 (See $#35B).

A bomb exploded outside an army barracks in the Lagos suburb of Yaba, on May 7, wounding five soldiers. Shortly afterwards a second explosion nearby caused considerable panic in the locality.  Five suspects were later detained. NADECO had been blamed for a series of anonymous bomb attacks on military personnel between December 1996 and May 1997 (See $#35B).

A journalist on the staff of the Lagos-based magazine Fame, named as Gareth Ateluye, was reported on Oct. 9 to have died after being released from police custody. Whilst in detention, according to the opposition Radio Kudirat, he had been tortured "to the point of death".
The Agence France-Presse (AFP) news agency reported on April 24 that nine people had been killed in two bomb attacks on April 23-24, in which one of these bombs went off in Lagos.

Armed confrontation between the security forces and Islamic extremists who were demanding the release of their spiritual leader, Ibrahim Yakub al-Zagzaki. At least eight people, including four policemen, were reportedly killed.[

The security forces dispersed an opposition protest in Lagos on June 12, the protesters were demanding the release of Gani Fawehinmi, a lawyer who had helped to organise the protests, was briefly arrested, as was a wife of Abiola.

Murder of a policeman and the destruction of two police stations on March 1 in rioting which was believed to have been connected with Obasanjo's victory in the elections. Six rioters were also reported to have been killed in the violence.

ELOCAL: Police barracks and ploice station in the outskirts
COMMENT: At least five policemen were killed on March 3 when rioters in Lagos attacked and badly damaged a police barracks and burnt down a police station on the city's outskirts. The attackers were thought to have been supporters of the defeated presidential candidate, Olu Falae, protesting against perceived electoral fraud.

Ten people died in Lagos on Sept. 9 during clashes between factions of the Nigerian DockWorkers Union (NDWU). The fighting was apparently caused by a dispute over election results to the leadership of the union.

Ethnic clashes between Yorubas and Hausas--Nigeria’s two largest ethnic groups--broke out in a market district of Lagos on Nov. 25-26.  At least 50 people died in the fighting.  President Olusegun Obasanjo ordered the police to shoot rioters on sight to restore order, and blamed the clashes on the hardline Yoruba nationalist group the Odua People’s Congress (OPC).  The OPC denied responsibility for the disorder

The Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) called a general strike on June 8 to protest against the price increases.  Initially, the strike drew in public sector and transport workers, but on June 9 blue and white-collar oil workers also joined the action. The strike was called off on June 13.

Clashes broke out in Lagos on Oct. 12 between the Odua People’s Congress (OPC)--the Yoruba separatist organisation--and Hausas .  Police, supported by troops, tried to quell the violence, but fighting flared up again on Oct. 18, with clashes spreading into the business district of Lagos before police and troops finally restored order. The Lagos State police estimated that 60 people were killed and 30 wounded in the clashes.  However, other estimates put the number of dead at over 100.

A series of explosions at an arms depot at the Ikeja army barracks in Lagos on Jan. 27 resulted in large numbers of civilian casualties.  By the end of the month 600 people were known to have died in the disaster, but a further 1,100 people were reported missing. The explosions began when a fire spread from a petrol station to the depot and detonated munitions there.  The blasts propelled shrapnel in all directions, destroying homes and businesses and shattering windows in offices some 15 km away.

A strike by police officers in the south-eastern Cross River state in late January spread to Lagos and a number of other cities on Feb. 1.  The police took the action in support of their demands for payment of wage arrears and for an improvement in working conditions.  The government said that it did not regard the action as a strike, but as a mutiny.  However, President Olusegun Obasanjo issued a statement on Feb. 1 saying that he had approved the immediate release of funds for the payment of outstanding police salaries.

A powerful explosion on Feb. 2 destroyed a bank and several other buildings in central Lagos, killing at least 40 people.  Looting and fights broke out after the blast, as hundreds of people fought over cash from the demolished bank.  Initially, the authorities said that the explosion was an accident, but it was later reclassified as a bombing after evidence of explosives was discovered at the scene.  A number of bank employees were arrested in connection with the explosion.

General strike called by the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) to protest at the government’s decision (taken on June 20) to increase the price of petrol by more than 50 per cent.  Ports, banks, shops, and petrol stations were closed, despite the police firing teargas and live rounds to disperse protesters in Abuja, the capital, and Lagos. The police battled with demonstrators in Abuja, the capital, and Lagos, the country’s commercial hub, on July 1, leaving at least eight people dead. Officials from the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) said on July 7 that the police had that day shot dead at least 10 protesters during violence in Lagos.
March to protest against the Obasanjo government, which they accused of being “a civilian dictatorship”.

NLC called a general strike on Oct. 11-14 to protest at rising fuel prices.  The strike was triggered by a rise in September of about 25 per cent.
The NLC and a wider coalition of civil society groups called another general strike on Oct. 31 against the fuel prices.

Police and army units fought running battles after a clash between a soldier and a policeman. Three people were killed in central Lagos. The unrest quickly spread through central Lagos, with gangs of “street boys” taking advantage of the chaos to loot and steal.
A ruptured oil pipeline exploded in Lagos, the commercial capital, on Dec. 26, killing at least 265 people and badly injuring many more.

COMMENT: Coup attempt. The coup attempt had been led by Capt. Stephen Lungu who, with a group of around 20 apparently drunk soldiers, took over the headquarters of the Zambian National Broadcasting Corporation (ZNBC) in Lusaka in the early hours of the morning. Broadcasting on the radio, Lungu introduced himself as Capt. Solo and announced that a previously unknown group calling itself the National Redemption Council had taken over the country. A few hours after the coup began loyal troops stormed the radio station and captured Lungu who was apparently found hiding in a rubbish bin. Defence Minister Ben Mwila subsequently reported that one solider had been killed and 15 others arrested in the operation.

Although there was immediate speculation that Wezi’s murder was politically motivated, initial police reports attributed the killing to “bandits”.
