Troops from the transitional government, along with Ethiopian soldiers, poured into the capital and quickly secured all important positions, including the port, the airport, and the presidential palace.
Anti-Ethiopian riots had erupted in the city prior to the visit of Ali Mohammed Gedi, Prime Minister of the transitional government.
In Bombay, work in all railway workshops except one was almost totally paralysed, and telegraph services were affected in Bombay, Agra, Nagpur, and Jaipur.
Bombay and other cities in Maharashtra were brought to a standstill by a general strike on Aug. 12.
Students entered the Belgian Consulate in Bombay for a sit-down demonstration.
A demonstration in Bombay city on May 21 was broken up by the police, who made a number of arrests.
About 15,000 people picketed the State Secretariat in Bombay on Aug. 24 as a protest against the State Government's failure to secure the implementation of the Legislature's resolution of April 5.
General strikes were organized by the left-wing parties in Bombay on Aug. 25.
Protest demonstrations against the report took place in Bombay and Belgaum on Nov. 5.
In Bombay demonstrators burnt copies of the Language Bill on Dec. 12 and destroyed English signs and literature.
An agitation by the Shiv Sena in support of the merger of Marathi-speaking areas of Mysore with Maharashtra led to violent rioting in Bombay on Feb. 7-11, 1969.
Similar one-day fasts were observed by thousands of people in Bombay, Patna, the Punjab, Hariana, and other parts of India.
An outbreak of communal rioting occurred in Bombay on Aug. 24, and after a lathi charge had failed to disperse the rioters the police opened fire, killing one person and wounding seven.

A general strike in Bombay on May 15, called by the left-wing parties as a protest against the State Government's failure to supply adequate foodgrains to ration shops, led to clashes between strikers and non-strikers.
The riots spread on Dec. 10 to Bombay.
A general strike in Bombay on Dec. 18, called by the Shiv Sena with the support of all the leading political parties, received almost total support
In Maharashtra a general strike on Jan. 2 called by the left-wing trade unions received total support in Bombay, Poona and other cities and towns.

Clashes between Dalit Panthers and caste Hindus began in central Bombay on Jan. 6, when the latter attacked a meeting organized by the Panthers in support of their boycott campaign, and continued at intervals throughout January and February.
Shiv Sena supporters demonstrated in Bombay on Jan. 25, demanding that 80 per cent of the posts in a nationalized bank should be reserved for people of Maharashtrian parentage
Clashes between Harijans and caste Hindus in Bombay.

A protest demonstration against food shortages in Bombay on Feb. 22, which had been organized by the Jan Sangh and other Opposition parties, developed into riots in which over 300 motor vehicles were damaged. The police opened fire several times, killing seven people.
In Bombay railway and bus workers went on strike as a protest against the death of a union leader who died of a heart attack shortly after his arrest.
A general strike called in Bombay on June 26 met with some support.
The most serious being a strike by Bombay port workers on May 9-19 in protest against the port authorities' failure to fill over 2,000 vacancies in their ranks.
In Bombay, where 25,000 workers in seven textile mills had been on strike in support of wage demands since Oct.20, 1981
A one-day general strike in Maharashtra on April 19 in sympathy with the textile workers received wide support in Bombay and most parts of the state.
The police revolt took place in Bombay on Aug. 18, 1982
The police revolt took place in Bombay on Aug. 18, 1982, coinciding with mass demonstrations by striking textile workers.

On May 20 six people died when the police opened fire on rioters in a suburb of Bombay and on the following day, as Mrs Gandhi visited the area, five more people were killed in similar circumstances. On May 24 it was reported that 21 people were killed when the police opened fire on rioters in different parts of Bombay and the Thane and Kalyan areas to the north-east of the city.
Two bombs exploded in Bombay on May 25 injuring at least 11 people.

Towards the end of June, following four deaths attributed to intercommunal strife in the Kherwadi district of Bombay, Hindus began street protests over the arrest on June 24 of a leader of the Hindu communalist
Mr Percy Norris, the British deputy high commissioner in Bombay, was shot dead by two unidentified assailants
At least 10 people were killed and more than 50 others injured in Bombay, India, on Feb. 24 after police opened fire on demonstrators at an anti-Rushdie rally.
Strikes against the government.

Numerous deaths and arson attacks on Hindu and Moslem religious shrines across India despite strict security arrangements in most states. Some of the worst affected cities were Bhopal, Bombay, Calcutta, Delhi, Jaipur, Kanpur and Surat.
Intense communal violence triggered by the destruction of the mosque at Ayodhya flared again in the western cities of Bombay and Ahmedabad from Jan. 5.
More than 250 people were killed and at least 1,200 injured in Bombay on March 12 when a synchronized chain of car bombs destroyed the city's stock exchange.
On Jan. 21 a strike by the right-wing opposition Shiv Sena, the only party to oppose the action, paralysed the state's commercial capital, Bombay, and triggered widespread caste violence.
The president of the Bombay branch of the pro-Hindu Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP), Ramdasa Naikwas shot dead in a western suburb of the city on Aug. 25.
Datta Samant, one of the country’s most prominent trade union leaders, was killed on Jan. 16 when four unidentified men ambushed his car near his Bombay home.
The killing led to a spontaneous strike and sporadic violence in the industrial belt of central and north Bombay coinciding with Samant’s funeral on Jan. 17.
Bombay police shot dead 11 people protesting at the desecration of a social activist's statue in the city on July 11.
On the eve of polling for the third phase of the election on Feb. 27, four people were killed by a series of bomb blasts in and around railway stations in Bombay.
On Dec. 2-3 Hindu extremists of the Shiv Sena organisation smashed up cinemas in New Delhi, Bombay, Surat and Pune
Police in Bombay, the capital of Maharashtra state, on March 6 arrested 100 Muslim activists protesting against the controversial self-exiled Bangladeshi writer Taslima Nasreen.
About 30 people were injured, eight critically, when two bombs exploded on Jan. 27 in Bombay while Prime Minister Vajpayee was visiting the city.
A bomb exploded on March 13 in a suburban commuter train in Bombay (Mumbai), the capital of western Maharashtra state, killing at least 12 people and injuring 75.
At least three people were killed and 30 injured on July 28 when a bomb exploded on a bus in the city of Bombay (Mumbai).
Two bombs concealed in taxis exploded on Aug. 25 in southern Bombay (Mumbai), the capital of western Maharashtra state, killing 52 people and injuring more than 150.
Sharon’s visit had provoked large demonstrations in the capital, New Delhi, Bombay, and in Kashmir and Calcutta, in support of the Palestinian cause.
Seven bombs exploding on July 11 within 15 minutes of each other on commuter trains and stations in the western city of Bombay (Mumbai),
A strike called by the N.P.C.P for Kenyatta's release was almost completely unsucessful in Nairobi.
KANU supporters wrecked the KADU office in Nairobi.
50 KANU backbenchers demonstrated with placards reading: "Kenya wants federation now", at the Conference on East African Federation.

Mr. Pia da Gama Pinto, editor of the militant Pan-Africa magazine in Nairobi and a specially elected Member of Parliament, who during the Mau Mau emergency had been the only Asian detainee was killed by three African gunmen outside his house in Nairobi on Feb. 24.
Left-wing demonstrators attempted to take over the KANU headquarters in Nairobi, but the attempt was foiled by the police and 26 men and a woman was arrested.
Assasination of Mr. Tom Mboya, Minister of Economic Planning, by a young African gunman, who fired two shots at point-blank range and succeeded in escaping from the scene of the murder.

Violent demonstrations took place at the Roman Catholic Cathedral in Nairobi at the requiem Mass for Mr. Mboy, but were quelled by police riot squads and units of the Kenya Army; unofficial reports said that three Kikuyu were killed and at least 60 persons injured.

Independence anniversary rally held in Nairobi on Dec. 12 President Kenyatta declared that his Government would not turn Kenya into a one-party State and would maintain the freedom of the judiciary as well as of the Press.
Assasination attempt on Mr Kariuki, the M.P. for Nyandarua North.

Bomb explosion at a long-distance bus terminus in central Nairobi, which had destroyed a crowded coach, killing 27 people and injuring many others. The bomb was generally assumed to have been planted by a group calling itself the Maskini L explosions in the capital during February.

Mr. Kariuki was last seen alive at a Nairobi hotel on the evening of March 2, and was reported missing to the police by his three wives on March 6. Circulation of rumours that Mr. Kariuki had been led away from the hotel at which he had last been seen by a member of the General Service Unit (Kenya's paramilitary security police force). Late on March 11 the Kariuki family announced that the missing M.P.'s body had been located in the Nairobi city mortuary, where it had remained since being found in woodland near the capital on March 3.

Nairobi University students boycotted classes to stage a number of demonstrations after the news about the murder of Mr Kariuki, but were dispersed without serious incident by riot police. Further minor demonstrations by students occurred on the following day as the boycott of classes continued, but otherwise conditions in Nairobi started to return to normal.

Violent demonstrations by students at Nairobi University on May 26, 1975-dispersed by riot police who fired shots into the air and made many arrests-were reported to be in protest against official attempts to mobilize pro-government opinion in the university with the aim of organizing a pledge of loyalty to President Kenyatta the name of the whole student body.
Two bomb explosions outside a Nairobi hotel, which caused some damage to cars and buildings.

Armed riot police were used on March 2, 1976, to form a cordon in front of Nairobi's Roman Catholic cathedral when thousands of people attended a requiem mass on the first anniversary of Mr Kariuki's disappearance (which still remained officially an unsolved crime). Students who jeered and taunted the police were dispersed with tear gas and batons, and 10 arrests were made, although a separate demonstration on the Nairobi University campus to commemorate the anniversary ended peacefully.
Professor Ngugi's release in particular prompted a large demonstration in favour of President Moi by Nairobi University students following the announcement of the freeing of detainees on Dec.12.

Bomb explosion occurred in Nairobi on Dec. 31, 1980, killing 15 people and injuring over 80 others at the city's prestigious Norfolk hotel.Kenyan Government announced on Jan. 7 that the bomb had been planted by Mr Qaddurah Mohammed Abdel Hamid, who was described as a PFLP member and Moroccan national travelling on Maltese papers.

ACTOR1: Members of the Kenyan Air Force
COMMENT: Coup attempt by members of the Kenyan Air Force, which was quickly defeated by loyal sections of the armed services and the paramilitary General Service Unit (GSU). The rebels, who were supported by some students, briefly seized Nairobi airport, the Voice of Kenya radio station, the telecommunications station and the post office, before encouraging widespread looting of shops in Nairobi.

Police used teargas on Feb. 10, 1985, to disperse a crowd of some 2,000 students at an outdoor interdenominational prayer meeting held at Nairobi University. Official statements recorded that one student was killed and 65 injured, but unofficial sources claimed that 12 had died.

The arrest of seven newly-elected leaders of the Student Organization of Nairobi University (SONU) led to riots which were vigorously suppressed by the paramilitary General Services Unit (GSU). Four foreign journalists who were found on the campus were also beaten by the GSU.
Violent clashes between police and 15,000 mourners at a funeral service for Ouko in Nairobi on Feb. 23.
The universitiy of Nairobi had been closed following confrontations between students and police.

Rally held on July 7 by advocates of political reform was attended by some 6,000 at a stadium in Nairobi. After the police broke up the meeting the demonstration turned to violence and looting; more than 1,000 people were arrested as rioting spread to other towns.

Three leading opposition figures were prevented from leaving Kenya in the first week of July to attend a human rights conference in London when their passports had been withdrawn by airport security officers.

About 50 women, demanding the release of 52 political prisoners, had begun a hunger strike in Nairobi's Uhuru Park on Feb. 28. The government, denying that it held any political prisoners, ordered the women to end their protest, saying that opposition supporters were taking advantage of it to cause unrest. The police action sparked two days of rioting by supporters of the women.

General strike. In Nairobi, the area most affected by the strike, commerce and transport were disrupted on both days. On April 2 the police used batons, tear gas and live ammunition to disperse hundreds of youths who had set up roadblocks and stoned and burned cars in shanty towns on the outskirts of the capital.

ELOCAL: Near the venue of the Odinga faction's congress
COMMENT: A series of bomb explosions on Sept. 3 near the venue of the Odinga faction's congress and the outbreak of sporadic fighting between supporters of the two groups sparked fears of tribal violence between the Luo (followers of Odinga), and the Kikuyu and Luhya (supporters of Matiba and Shikuku respectively)
Anti-KANU demonstration which was dispersed by riot police.

Two leading opposition MPs were among a group of six people arrested by police during a raid on the Nairobi offices of the Mwangaza Trust on April 4. In January the government had revoked the charitable status of the Trust, effectively banning it, declaring it to be a political party

Three days of rioting in Kibera, a large Nairobi slum, on Oct. 16-18. Violence had erupted after the mutilated body of a Nubian youth was found in a Luo area of Kibera. At least 7 people were reported killed.

Violent protests in Nairobi on Feb. 23-26 after death the of a student leader. Angry students accused the police of direct involvement in his death. The protests brought the main government area of the capital to a standstill as they erected barricades and burnt cars.

The charred body of Solomon Muruli had been found in his room at a Nairobi University hostel on Feb. 23. Witnesses said that fire had swept through the room following an explosion. Despite protests from foreign diplomats, human rights organisations and opposition groups, the police said that they did not regard Muruli’s death as murder. Angry students accused the police of direct involvement in his death.
Opposition rally in Uhuru Park, central Nairobi, on May 31. which was broken off by the police. Riots and looting were reported in the city centre.
Soldiers and riot police dispersed crowds of demonstrators from the streets of Nairobi on June 19 and later clashed with hundreds of students elsewhere in the capital.

Pro-democracy rallies across the country. The rallies had been organised by opposition parties, religious leaders and civil groups as part of their campaign for democratic reform in advance of elections due to be held before the end of the year. in Nairobi's All Saints Cathedral where police fired teargas and beat worshippers with pickaxe handles. Two men were apparently beaten to death as they knelt near the altar. Rev. Timothy Njoya, moderator of the Presbyterian Church of East Africa, was critically injured despite raising his hands in surrender. On July 9 the University of Nairobi and the Jomo Kenyatta University of Agriculture and Technology were closed after three days of student unrest during which two students were reportedly shot dead.

On July 14-15 thousands of students demanding democratic reform clashed with riot police in central Nairobi, with students seizing control of Haile Selassie Avenue, a main thoroughfare. Police once again used live rounds to break up the protests.

One-day general strike on Aug. 8 by the National Convention Executive Council (NCEC), a formation of opposition parties and pro-reform groups. The action was in defiance of a government ban and resulted in violence in Nairobi. a number of violent incidents were reported as demonstrators clashed with police. In the worst of the violence two policemen and three protesters were killed in outbreaks of fighting in central Nairobi and at Kiambu, 10 km to the north of the capital.

More than 287,000 primary and secondary school teachers began a strike on Oct. 1, called by the Kenya National Union of Teachers (KNUT) in support of demands for salary increases of 150-200 percent. The strike was called off October 12.

Violence broke out when the NCEC attempted to stage a rally at the Kamakunji sports ground, on the outskirts of Nairobi, on Oct. 10. The rally had been organised to commemorate approximately 100 Kenyans who, it was said, had died in political violence during the year. The rally coincided with official celebrations marking Moi's 19th year in power, and was seen by observers as a deliberate snub to the President. It was broken up by police who used teargas, batons and live ammunition to disperse the protesters.

A group of opposition legislators heckled President Daniel arap Moi as he arrived for the first session of the new National Assembly. Some 50 MPs chanted and waved placards protesting at alleged "genocide" and a "legacy of killing", following the recent deaths of around 100 people in Rift Valley Province.

ACTOR1: Senior figures in the Roman Catholic church
COMMENT: On Feb. 11 senior figures in the Roman Catholic church, joined by some opposition legislators, led a peaceful march through central Nairobi (the capital) and held a prayer-meeting in Uhuru Park to protest against killings in the Rift Valley.

An estimated 12,000 bank employees went on strike on Feb. 27 after the Finance Ministry announced that it was imposing a 22 percent tax on concessionary loans to which the employees were eligible. on March 4 the Finance Minister, Simeon Nyachae, suspended the tax pending a legislative review. Consequently, on March 5 the secretary general of COTU, Joseph Mugalla, called off the threatened general strike.

Assasination of Seth Sendashonga, a former Rwandan Interior Minister and leading Rwandan opposition figure was shot dead in his car in Nairobi, on May 16. A fellow Hutu, Bosco Kulyubukeye, who like Sendashonga was employed by the UN Environment Programme (UNEP), was also killed in the attack. a Rwandan Tutsi businessman living in Nairobi, motivated by personal revenge, had confessed to ordering Sendashonga's murder. Two other men, a Rwandan and a Ugandan, were also reported to have been arrested in connection with the killings.

Students rioted in Nairobi. They broke office windows, stoned cars and looted shops before police restored order. In a speech the same day, President Daniel arap Moi accused the National Convention Executive Council (NCEC) of planning to incite students to violence.

Co-ordinated bomb attacks were carried out on the US embassies in Nairobi and Dar es Salaam on Aug. 7. At least 253 people were killed in Nairobi, including 12 US citizens, and around 5,000 others were injured.

During February students at the Nairobi and Kenyatta Universities led protests against plans to redevelop the protected Karura forest on the outskirts of Nairobi, the capital. The demonstrators opposed plans to construct a luxury housing estate in the government-owned forest.

ACTOR2: Opposition members of the national assembly
COMMENT: Church leaders, opposition members of the National Assembly, and others were reportedly attacked by riot police on June 10 as they gathered outside the Assembly in Nairobi (the capital) to protest against the decision that a proposed review of the constitution was to be carried out solely by the National Assembly.

A rally of 10,000 people by opposition groups calling for a nationwide strike to force Moi's government to resign was held on July 7 in Nairobi, the capital. It coincided with a two-day strike by minibus operators in which at least 320 people were arrested in street fighting and one man was shot by the police.
Hundreds of people marched through central Nairobi on Aug. 30 in protest of the murder of a Roman Catholic priest, Fr John Kaiser, in what many alleged was a political murder.

TARGET1: Director of a small chemical company
COMMENT: It was reported in mid-November that over 120 people had died in the capital Nairobi after drinking chang’aa, an illicit gin-like drink, laced with industrial methanol.  Some 400 other people had been hospitalised, and doctors warned that many of the injured would die or be permanently blinded.  The Guardian of Nov. 18 reported that some 6,000 Kenyans had died in 1999 as a result of drinking illegal spirits. A police spokesman said on Nov. 19 that 22 people had been arrested in connection with the deaths, including the director of a small chemical company.

At least 12 people were killed and many more were injured in the capital, Nairobi, on Dec. 3-4 as hostility between landlords and tenants grew into serious ethnic clashes. The violence erupted on Dec. 3 when a family of the Luo tribe refused to pay rent to their Nubian landlord, and beat him up.  Nubians responded by attacking the homes of Luo residents in the Kiberia area of Nairobi, killing 12 people.
The police were forced to use teargas to disperse students protesting in the centre of the capital, Nairobi, on July 19, in a second day of rioting following the killing of a student.

Crispin Odhiambo Mbai, head of the political science department of Nairobi University, was killed in mid-September in what appeared to be a political assassination.  Mbai had been involved in negotiating a new constitution designed to curb presidential and state powers.
Student riots and unrest in Nairobi was sparked by the assasination of Crispin Odhiambo Mbai (See $#43A).

Serious rioting broke out in the capital, Nairobi, and the western city of Kisumu on July 3 after paramilitary riot police attempted to enforce a ban on anti-government protests. The demonstrators were protesting at President Kibaki’s failure to press ahead with promised reforms, including the promulgation of a new constitution that would reduce the powers of the presidency.

Armed police officers wearing masks on March 2 stormed the printing plant of The Standard in the capital, Nairobi, damaging the presses and setting fire to thousands of copies of the March 2 edition. A second police squad targeted the offices of the Standard Group’s 24-hour television station, Kenya Television Network (KTN), destroying equipment and forcing the station off air for 12 hours.
The Akali demonstration in Delhi on June 12, which had been officially prohibited, developed into violent rioting. One Akali was killed
11 Sikhs peacefully courted arrest in Delhi on June 19, which was observed by the Akali Dal as a day of protest against alleged police excesses against the demonstrators
Strike by Government employees, only limited support in Delhi

Police cordoned a shrine in Delhi inside which thousands of Sikhs had assembled in preparation for a march to the Parliament building. The Sikhs threw stones over the walls at the police, who replied by firing tear-gas shells into the grounds of the shrine
Women M.P.s staged demonstrations in Delhi against the "watering down" of  "The Dowry Prohibition Act"
Protest against the continued use of English as an official language of the Indian Union
Attacks on British monuments in April
Members of the extremist pro-Hindi Jan Sangh in New Delhi on Feb. 20 smeared tar on signs written in English

Rioting spread to Old Delhi on March 14 when the Jan Sangh declared a hartal and tried to impose it by coercion. Many shopkeepers resisted and fighting broke out between Jan Sangh members and followers of Master Tara Singh, over 100 people being injured
In New Delhi sadhus fasted outside the homes of Ministers from July onwards as a protest against the Government's refusal to introduce a total ban on cow slaughter
Peaceful demonstration organized by the right-wing Communist Party outside the Houses of Parliament in New Delhi on Sept. 1 to protest against rising prices, food shortages
Agitation by Hindu communal organizations in support of a total ban on cow slaughter led to violent rioting in New Delhi on Nov. 7 in which eight people were killed
25 people were injured in fighting between rival party groups in Old Delhi
Demonstrations organized by the Jan Sangh, which began outside the Chinese Embassy on June 14, developed into a serious riot two days later
Anti-English demonstrations
Demonstrations outside Parliament
Demonstrations against Soviet arms sales to Pakistan
Strike of Government Employees for higher wages
Protest demonstration by over 20,000 workers outside the Prime Minister's residence against the sacking of on nearly 48,000 temporary Government employees after the strike in Sept.
Demonstration organized by the Delhi State Congress Committee
On Nov. 17 about 500,000 people from Hariana marched through New Delhi, calling for that the city Chandigarh should be the capital of Hariana

ACTOR1: Chairman of the Hariana Forward Bloc
COMMENT: Chairman of the Hariana Forward Bloc began a fast on Jan. 26 outside Mrs. Gandhi's residence in New Delhi, and announced that he would burn himself to death on Feb. 1 if Chandigarh were not granted to Hariana
A crowd estimated at 10,000 people, led by students, demonstrated outside the Pakistan High Commission in New Delhi on Feb. 3-5 after the destruction of a hijacked Indian air plane
Anti-Chinese demonstrations on March 29 outside the Chinese Embassy in New Delhi

The section of the Akali Dal led by Sant Fateh Singh launched an agitation on Aug. 15 to protest against the formation of the Delhi Gurdwara Board by the Union Government, following a dispute between two factions over the management of a gurdwara (Sikh shrine) in Delhi.
Agitation by Akali faction
Agitation by Akali faction
Mass meeting in New Delhi addessed by Mrs. Gandhi
The Jan Sangh organized a protest march against the Simla Agreement
Rioting broke out in the New Delhi suburb of Shahdara on Aug. 19
Rioting by Harijans ("untouchables") occurred in Old Delhi on Sept. 9-12, after a Harijan schoolgirl had been found dead
Lawyers in most courts throughout India boycotted the courts on May 3 as a protest against the appointment of a new Chief Justice of the Supreme Court
Communal riots described as the worst since independence occurred I Old Delhi on May 5, 1974, at least 10 people being killed and over 300 injured. Following a quarrel between a Hindu and a Moslem
The longest railway strike in Indian history began on May 8 and ended on May 28. In the metropolitan areas of Delhi, Calcutta, Bombay and Madras, however, services came virtually to a standstill
Opposition supporters staged a protest demonstration outside the Bangladesh High Commission in New Delhi on Jan.30
Ten people were killed and about 100 injured on Feb. 2, 1975, during riots around the Jama Masjid, Delhi's most important mosqu
Mr. Narayan and leaders of the non-communist opposition parties led a huge demonstration which marched through Delhi on March 6
An attempt was made on the life of the Chief Justice of India, Mr. Ajit Nath Ray, in New Delhi on March 20
A mass demonstration of support for Mrs. Gandhi was organized in Delhi on June 20
Opposition demonstrations on June 25, 29 and July 4
A fire which broke out in All-India Radio's television studios on June 28 was attributed by the radio to sabotage

Slum clearance operations in Old Delhi gave rise to riots on April 19, 1976, six people being killed when the police opened fire; the riots were attributed to rumours that the people being rehoused would be compulsorily sterilized. one witness suggested that the number killed was not less than 20
Small local demonstrations
Mrs Gandhi's arrest was followed by widespread riots and demonstrations in her support
Mass peasant demonstration in Delhi on Dec. 23, 1977 to mark Mr Charan Singh's 76th birthday

Over 10,000 farmers demonstrated against the grant outside the Prime Minister's residence in New Delhi on Aug.13, 1978. Protest demonstrations by farmers nevertheless continued in New Delhi during the next two months
Arson attack against the All-India Radio building in New Delhi on Nov.25
Mass peasant demonstration in Delhi on Dec. 23, 1978 to mark Mr Charan Singh's 77th birthday
Attacks on the Nirankaris by orthodox Sikhs, who regard them as heretics, led to riots in Amritsar, Kanpur and New Delhi in 1978 in which 34 people were killed

Protest demonstration against by Youth Congress members led by Mr Sanjay Gandhi led to violent rioting in New Delhi on May 1, many people were injured and Mr Gandhi and about 400 of his supporters were arrested
Mass protest demonstration by Christians in New Delhi on May 14, 1979
A "people's march" of Congress supporters in New Delhi un May 16, which was addressed by Mrs Gandhi and attended by about 50,000 people, passed off generally peacefully
The Punjab police staged a series of demonstrations from May 7 onwards in support of demands for improved pay and conditions and the right to form a union, the demonstrations spread to Delhi
The Central Reserve Police (CRP) went on strike on June 21, the army took control over a CRP campon the outskirts of New Delhi on June 25 killing three CRP men
Afghan students on Jan. 2 seized their country's embassy in New Delhi in protest at the Soviet invasion, holding several members of staff captive for some hours
On March 16 police attacked a procession of blind people who were on their way to present a petition to Mrs Gandhi,
An apparent attempt on Mrs Gandhi's life was made in New Delhi on April 14, when a man threw a knife at her
The leader of the Nirankari Sikhs, and his bodyguard were shot dead outside his home in New Delhi on April 24 by orthodox Sikhs

Fighting broke out in New Delhi on Aug. 13 following a dispute between the congregation of a mosque and Harijans, seven people being seriously injured. On the following day Moslems stoned and set fire to Hindu shops in Old Delhi, and two people were killed when the police opened fire
The first secretary in the Kuwaiti embassy was shot dead on June 4, 1981
On Sept. 12 a number of people including 60 policemen were injured in a demonstration by the members of the reformist Nirankari Sikh sect

Demonstration on Oct. 11 outside the Parliament building in New Delhi. Police opened fire on a crowd of about 3,000 rioting protesters who were reportedly trying to force their way into the Lok Sabha; four people were killed and a fifth died later in hospital
The Jordanian ambassador to India, was shot and seriously wounded by an unknown assailant in New Delhi on Oct. 25, 1983
A grenade had been thrown at the Soviet embassy in New Delhi, the Indian capital, causing some damage but no injury
Akali Dal leaders burned copies of the Indian constitution to draw attention to their demand that the Sikh religion be recognized as fully distinct from Hinduism
Much of New Delhi was brought to a halt when the BJP called a strike to protest against the Sikh demonstrators in February
In New Delhi on March 28 a prominent moderate Sikh, died after being shot by a group of young Sikhs
A demonstration involving 2,000 Hindus took place on May 28 outside the Bangladesh embassy in New Delhi. One person was killed and two injured when police opened fire on the crowd
In New Delhi two people died after angry Sikhs poured out of a temple and confronted the police
Protest demonstrations were held outside the British high commission in New Delhi, and a crowd threw stones at the New Delhi home of a BBC correspondent
Opposition rally
Five people had died in Delhi when bombs exploded at two cinemas on Oct. 13. Akali leaders condemned the sabotage, but it was widely assumed that Sikh militants were responsible

Following Mrs Gandhi's assassination on Oct. 31 by Sikh members of her bodyguard, the union territory of Delhi, were swept by the worst violence since Partition in 1947. Official figures released on Jan. 24, 1985, put the total number of people killed during the violence estimated at between 2,307 and 3,874 in Delhi. many participants in the riots came from the lower ranks of the Congress and a commission report published in 2005 found that local Congress politicians had played a role in organising the attacks on Sikhs
On Oct. 31, 1984, Mrs Indira Gandhi, the Prime Minister of India, was assassinated in New Delhi by two Sikh members of her bodyguard
Demonstrations for peace
Demonstration in front of the Congress headquarters in New Delhi by some members of the Congress Youth
A senior engineer in the council for economic affairs at the Soviet embassy in New Delhi, India, was shot dead on March 21, 1985

A number of bombs exploded in New Delhi and nearby towns on May 10-11. The bombs were concealed in transistor radios which were then left in bus stations, on trains and in other public places. More than 80 people were killed in the explosions
A Congress member of the Lok Sabha, was shot and killed at his Delhi home on July 31. His wife and a friend were also killed in the attack
A prominent Congress figure in the Delhi Metropolitan Council, was shot and killed in Delhi on Sept. 4
Strikes organized by opposition parties in protest at the increase in food prices took place on Feb. 10 and 11 in New Delhi
March on parliament to protest against the rise in food prices, 10 000 people detained
6 people died during demonstrations
Riots in New Delhi in response to Sikhs killing a least eight Hindus north of Amritsar
African students clashed with police in New Delhi, on Feb. 27, 1987, during demonstrations against a government decision of Jan. 3, 1987, to introduce compulsory AIDS tests for foreign students

100-300 people died during clashes between Hindus and Moslems in Uttar Pradesh and Delhi between May 18-26, 1987… unrest in the city's old sector and sporadic violence in the city eventually resulted in eight deaths by May 25
Two Sikh extremists killed at least 12 people and injured over 20 in four separate attacks in southern Delhi on June 13
African students clashed with police in New Delhi on July 13, during demonstrations against a government decision of Jan. 3, 1987, to introduce compulsory AIDS tests for foreign students
